# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**Q: Differentiate between throwaway prototyping and evolutionary prototyping. Which is more suitable for a CPS interface in the early design phase, and why?**

**Model Answer:**

Throwaway prototyping is a technique where a quick, low-fidelity model is built primarily to learn about the problem domain and user expectations. Once the required learnings are extracted, the prototype is **discarded**, and the actual system is built from scratch using the insights gained. It is characterized by low code quality, minimal documentation, and rapid iteration. The focus is on *exploration*, not on producing production-ready artefacts.

Evolutionary prototyping, in contrast, involves building a working model that is **continuously refined and extended** until it gradually becomes the final system. The prototype is built with modular architecture and production-quality code from the start. It is suitable when requirements are well-understood and the goal is incremental delivery.

For a CPS interface in the **early design phase**, **throwaway prototyping is more suitable** because:
- Requirements are typically ambiguous and not yet stable.
- The team needs to explore multiple HMI layouts, sensor placement strategies, and interaction patterns.
- Building a throwaway prototype on paper or in Figma costs almost nothing, while committing to a high-fidelity evolutionary prototype at this stage is premature.
- The risk of locking in a wrong design is minimized because the prototype is explicitly intended to be discarded.

Thus, throwaway prototyping aligns with the *exploratory* nature of early CPS interface design, where validated design knowledge — not the prototype itself — is the true deliverable.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]

**Q: What is a Wizard of Oz prototype? Give an example of its application in a CPS interface.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Wizard of Oz prototype** is a prototyping technique in which the user interacts with what appears to be a fully autonomous CPS interface, but the system's responses are actually generated by a **hidden human operator** (the "wizard"). The user is unaware of the human behind the curtain. This technique is named after the famous scene in *The Wizard of Oz*, where the apparent omnipotence of the wizard is revealed to be a man operating controls behind a screen.

The purpose of this technique is to **simulate the behavior of an AI, ML, or natural-language processing component** that has not yet been implemented. It allows designers to validate user expectations, refine interaction patterns, and gather training data for the eventual algorithmic implementation, all without committing to the cost and complexity of building the actual intelligent system.

**Example in CPS Interface:** Consider a smart-home CPS where the user speaks commands like *"Lower the blinds and dim the living room lights."* The natural-language understanding algorithm has not been developed yet. A Wizard of Oz prototype would have the user speak to a microphone-equipped interface, while a hidden operator listens and manually triggers the corresponding actuator commands via a separate control panel. The user perceives the system as fully voice-controlled, and the design team can observe which phrasings, accents, and command structures users naturally employ — informing the training of the eventual NLP model.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often write only the *definition* of a Wizard of Oz prototype and forget to **give a CPS-specific example**. In a 3-mark question, the *example carries 1.5 marks*. Always ground the definition in a concrete CPS scenario.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]

**Q: (a) [7 Marks] Explain in detail the various fidelity levels of prototypes used in CPS interface design. For each level, provide one example technique and one typical CPS use case.**

**(b) [7 Marks] Compare horizontal and vertical prototyping. A team is designing the operator dashboard for a nuclear reactor CPS. They need to validate the complete alarm-handling workflow from sensor detection to operator acknowledgment to actuator shutdown. Recommend either a horizontal or vertical prototype, and justify your choice.**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The fidelity level of a prototype refers to the **degree to which the prototype resembles the final system** in terms of visual finish, interactivity, functional depth, and physical realism. The four commonly recognized fidelity levels are:

**1. Low-Fidelity Prototypes**
- **Description:** Rough, abstract representations that focus on structure and flow rather than detail. They are quick to build, easy to modify, and inexpensive.
- **Example Technique:** Paper prototyping or low-fidelity wireframing in Balsamiq.
- **CPS Use Case:** Sketching the layout of a wearable health-monitor display (heart rate, SpO₂, step count) on paper to validate information hierarchy and button placement before any digital design work begins.
- **Advantages:** Forces focus on layout and logic, not aesthetics. Zero cost. Encourages frequent changes.
- **Limitations:** Cannot test real physical interaction, sensor feel, or visual polish.

**2. Medium-Fidelity Prototypes**
- **Description:** Digital, semi-interactive prototypes that approximate the visual design and basic interaction flows. Some animations and state transitions may be included.
- **Example Technique:** Interactive Figma prototype with click-through navigation.
- **CPS Use Case:** Building a click-through prototype of a smart-factory dashboard to test whether operators can navigate between machine-status screens, alarm panels, and maintenance logs using only mouse/touch input.
- **Advantages:** Allows realistic usability testing of HMI without writing firmware. Easy to share with remote stakeholders.
- **Limitations:** No real sensor or actuator data. Latency, noise, and physical ergonomics cannot be evaluated.

**3. High-Fidelity Prototypes**
- **Description:** Functional prototypes that closely resemble the final system. They use real (or near-final) hardware, firmware, sensors, and actuators. Visual design is production-grade.
- **Example Technique:** Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) prototype with real microcontroller, sensors, and HMI touchscreen.
- **CPS Use Case:** Validating an automotive ADAS interface on a test bench with real radar and camera sensors feeding live data to a production-style dashboard display, allowing engineers to study driver behavior under realistic latency and noise conditions.
- **Advantages:** Captures real-world phenomena. High ecological validity. Directly supports regulatory usability evidence.
- **Limitations:** Expensive, time-consuming, and risky to modify.

**4. Very High-Fidelity (Digital Twin) Prototypes**
- **Description:** A virtual replica of the physical CPS, fed by real-time sensor data, used to test scenarios that would be too dangerous, expensive, or slow to test in reality.
- **Example Technique:** Digital twin of a wind turbine CPS used to validate operator interfaces for emergency shutdown.
- **CPS Use Case:** Training nuclear reactor operators on a digital twin of the control room before the physical control room is commissioned.
- **Advantages:** Enables testing of hazardous scenarios with zero physical risk. Supports operator training.
- **Limitations:** Requires high-fidelity models of the physical plant. High upfront cost.

**Synthesis:** The choice of fidelity is dictated by the **design question** and the **stage of the project**. KTU examiners expect students to explicitly link the fidelity choice to the *question being answered*, not to a generic preference.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Comparison — Horizontal vs. Vertical Prototyping**

| Dimension | Horizontal Prototype | Vertical Prototype |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Wide — covers many features | Narrow — covers one feature |
| **Depth** | Shallow — each feature minimally implemented | Deep — one feature fully implemented |
| **Purpose** | Validate breadth: feature coverage, navigation | Validate depth: end-to-end functionality |
| **Risk Exposure** | Low — easy to discard and rework | Higher — deeper investment in one feature |
| **Typical Use** | UI navigation, information architecture | Critical-path functionality, performance validation |

**Recommendation for the Nuclear Reactor CPS Alarm-Handling Workflow:**

A **vertical prototype** is the correct choice for this scenario.

**Justification:**

1. **Safety-Critical Nature:** A nuclear reactor CPS operates in a safety-critical domain. The alarm-handling workflow (sensor detection → alarm generation → operator display → operator acknowledgment → actuator shutdown) must be validated **end-to-end**. A horizontal prototype covering many features superficially would not reveal critical issues such as sensor noise causing false alarms, latency in operator acknowledgment, or actuator response time during emergency shutdown.

2. **Depth of Validation Required:** The design question — *"Can the operator reliably detect, acknowledge, and act on a critical alarm within the regulatory time window?"* — is fundamentally a depth question. It requires testing the **complete stack** of one feature, including physical sensor behavior, embedded firmware timing, HMI display latency, and actuator response.

3. **Compliance Requirements:** Nuclear regulatory standards (e.g., IEC 61513, IEEE 603) require documented evidence of end-to-end functional validation. A vertical prototype generates exactly this type of evidence by exercising the full signal path from physical phenomenon to physical corrective action.

4. **Risk Reduction:** By investing depth in the *most safety-critical* feature first, the team surfaces the most serious design risks early, when changes are still cheap to make. The remaining features can then be added using horizontal-prototype breadth in later iterations.

A horizontal prototype would be appropriate at an *earlier* stage, when the team is still deciding *which* features to include in the operator dashboard. But once the alarm-handling workflow is identified as the critical path, the prototype must go vertical.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** The most common mistake students make is recommending a *horizontal* prototype simply because it "covers more features." This loses 3–4 marks. The correct reasoning is: *vertical prototyping is chosen because the question demands depth, not breadth*. Always justify with the **design question**, not with a generic preference.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative to Question A) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]

**Q: (a) [7 Marks] With a neat block diagram, explain the Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) prototyping approach for CPS interfaces. List two advantages and two limitations.**

**(b) [7 Marks] A startup is building a voice-controlled CPS for elderly patients to call a caregiver, adjust room temperature, and control lights. The voice-recognition algorithm is not yet developed. Recommend the most appropriate prototyping technique, justify your choice, and outline the steps to build and test the prototype.**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) Prototyping — Block Diagram:**

The HIL prototype consists of three major subsystems: the **Physical Subsystem** (the actual plant or process being controlled), the **Sensor and Actuator Layer** (real hardware interfacing with the physical process), and the **Embedded Controller and Interface** (the cyber side, including the microcontroller, firmware, and HMI).

```
+----------------------------------------------------------+
|                  PHYSICAL SUBSYSTEM                       |
|     (e.g., DC Motor, Pump, Thermal Chamber)              |
+----------------------------+-----------------------------+
                             |
              +--------------+--------------+
              |                             |
       SENSORS (T, P, Flow)         ACTUATORS (Motor, Valve)
              |                             ^
              v                             |
+----------------------------------------------------------+
|              EMBEDDED CONTROLLER LAYER                    |
|   ADC --> Microcontroller --> Firmware --> HMI Display   |
+----------------------------------------------------------+
              |                             ^
              +---------->  Operator  <-----+
                            (Human-in-Loop)
```

**Explanation:**

In an HIL prototype, the **physical plant is real** — for example, a DC motor driving a conveyor belt in a smart factory, or a thermal chamber simulating a chemical reactor. The **sensors** (temperature, pressure, flow) are physically mounted on the plant, and their analog signals are fed into an **ADC** and then into a **microcontroller** (such as ESP32, STM32, or Arduino). The **firmware** running on the microcontroller implements the control logic and updates the **HMI display** (touchscreen, web dashboard, or industrial panel).

The operator observes the HMI, makes control decisions, and sends commands back through the HMI, which the firmware translates into actuator commands (e.g., motor speed, valve position). This closes the **control loop**, which is the defining characteristic of a CPS.

**Advantages of HIL Prototyping:**

1. **Realistic Physical Behavior:** Real sensors exhibit noise, drift, and non-linearity; real actuators exhibit latency, backlash, and thermal effects. HIL captures all of these, producing a prototype with high ecological validity.
2. **End-to-End Validation:** The complete signal path — from physical phenomenon to computational decision to physical action — is exercised, enabling early detection of integration issues that would otherwise emerge only during field deployment.

**Limitations of HIL Prototyping:**

1. **Cost and Time:** HIL prototypes require real hardware (sensors, actuators, MCUs, enclosures, wiring), which is expensive and time-consuming to procure and integrate.
2. **Risk of Damage:** Real actuators and physical plants can be damaged during testing, especially when the control firmware has bugs. In safety-critical domains (medical, nuclear, aerospace), this risk may be unacceptable, making digital twin prototyping a safer alternative.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Recommended Prototyping Technique: Wizard of Oz Prototype**

**Justification:**

The CPS described is a **voice-controlled system for elderly patients**, with three target commands: call a caregiver, adjust room temperature, and control lights. The **voice-recognition algorithm has not yet been developed** — this is the central constraint.

A **Wizard of Oz prototype** is the most appropriate technique because:

1. **It simulates the unavailable AI.** The voice-recognition NLP algorithm is exactly the kind of intelligent component that Wizard of Oz prototyping is designed to emulate. A hidden human operator can interpret the patient's speech and manually trigger the corresponding action, making the system appear fully voice-controlled to the patient.

2. **It validates user expectations with the actual end-user.** Elderly patients are a user group with specific needs (speech patterns, accents, command phrasing, accessibility). The Wizard of Oz setup allows the design team to observe *how real elderly patients naturally phrase their requests*, generating critical data for training the eventual NLP model.

3. **It avoids premature algorithm investment.** Building, training, and validating a voice-recognition algorithm is expensive and time-consuming. A Wizard of Oz prototype defers this investment until the design team has empirical evidence that the *interaction design itself* is sound.

4. **It enables safety validation.** For an elderly-care application, the caregiver-call feature is safety-critical. The Wizard of Oz prototype allows testing of this critical path without risking algorithmic failure.

**Steps to Build and Test the Prototype:**

**Step 1 — Set up the controlled environment.** Create a room mock-up that resembles the patient's actual living space, with controllable lights (smart bulbs or manually switched lamps) and a thermostat (or a thermostat-like mockup).

**Step 2 — Provide the patient with a voice interface.** Use a simple microphone-and-speaker setup (e.g., a tablet running a basic voice-capture app, or a dedicated smart speaker enclosure) so the patient can speak commands naturally.

**Step 3 — Position the hidden "wizard."** Place a human operator in an adjacent room with audio monitoring. The wizard listens to the patient's commands in real time.

**Step 4 — Define the command-response mapping.** The wizard maintains a simple script:
- *"Call the caregiver"* → Trigger a simulated call notification to a researcher's phone.
- *"Increase/decrease the temperature"* → Manually adjust the thermostat or the mockup's temperature display.
- *"Turn on/off the lights"* → Manually toggle the smart bulbs or lamps.

**Step 5 — Recruit representative test users.** Enroll 5–8 elderly patients as test participants. Ensure diversity in age, accent, speech clarity, and familiarity with technology.

**Step 6 — Conduct the test sessions.** For each participant:
- Brief them that the system "responds to voice commands" (without revealing the wizard).
- Ask them to perform a set of scripted tasks (e.g., "Call your caregiver") and a set of unscripted tasks (e.g., "Make yourself more comfortable").
- Record all spoken commands, all system responses, and any user confusion, frustration, or delight.
- Measure task completion rate, time-on-task, and error rate.

**Step 7 — Analyze the data.** Extract patterns:
- What phrasings did users naturally use? (e.g., "Call my daughter" vs. "Initiate caregiver contact")
- How often did users rephrase or abandon commands?
- Did any commands reveal unanticipated needs (e.g., "I'm cold and I need help" — a request that combines temperature and caregiver functions)?

**Step 8 — Feed insights into the NLP training set.** Use the collected command phrasings as a foundational corpus for training the eventual voice-recognition model. Also feed insights into the HMI redesign (e.g., adding a confirmation prompt for safety-critical commands).

**Step 9 — Iterate.** Refine the prototype based on learnings, and re-test with a new cohort of users. Continue until $\vert L_i \vert < \epsilon$ — i.e., no new usability issues emerge across two consecutive test sessions.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** In a 14-mark question involving a *recommendation*, students often write only the *name* of the technique and lose 4–5 marks. The marks are distributed as: **Name (2 marks)**, **Justification (3 marks)**, **Steps (2 marks)**. Every component must be explicitly present in the answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

The following is a high-density, rapid-revision checklist for last-minute KTU exam preparation on this topic:

- **CPS Interface = Cyber (computation, communication) + Physical (sensors, actuators) + Interaction (HMI, operator).** Prototyping must address all three layers.
- **The purpose of prototyping is to *answer a design question*, not to *build the final product*.** The validated design knowledge is the deliverable, not the prototype itself.
- **Fidelity levels:** Low (paper, wireframe, storyboard) → Medium (mockup, interactive prototype, Wizard of Oz) → High (breadboard, HIL, digital twin). Always link fidelity to the *design question*.
- **Throwaway vs. Evolutionary:** Throwaway = *build, learn, discard*. Evolutionary = *build, refine, deploy*. Throwaway is for unclear requirements; evolutionary is for well-understood requirements.
- **Horizontal vs. Vertical:** Horizontal = *wide and shallow* (many features, little depth). Vertical = *narrow and deep* (one feature, full stack). Choose vertical for safety-critical or performance-critical features.
- **Wizard of Oz** is specifically for simulating unavailable AI/ML/NLP components. Always give a CPS-specific example in the exam.
- **HIL prototyping** uses *real physical hardware* with a real microcontroller and firmware, but the control system itself may still be evolving. HIL is the gold standard for end-to-end functional validation.
- **Digital twin** prototyping is used when the physical scenario is *hazardous, expensive, or slow* to test (e.g., nuclear reactors, autonomous vehicles, smart grids).
- **Usability metrics to remember:** SUS (0–100, >68 = above average), Task Completion Rate (%), Time-on-Task (seconds), Error Rate (per 100 interactions), Latency (ms).
- **Nielsen's heuristic:** 5–8 users are sufficient to surface 80–85% of usability issues. Use this number in test-planning answers.
- **Iterative convergence** is governed by $\vert L_i \vert < \epsilon$, where $L_i$ is the learning at iteration $i$ and $\epsilon$ is the convergence threshold.
- **Always justify your choice** of prototyping technique by linking it to the design question. A bare "use X" without justification will lose marks.
- **Storyboards** capture *context of use* (industrial floor, hospital, vehicle cockpit) and are essential for communicating CPS scenarios to multidisciplinary teams.
- **The prototyping process is iterative, not linear:** Build → Test → Learn → Refine → Repeat. The loop is the point.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Strategy Tip:** When a 14-mark question asks you to *recommend* a prototyping technique, structure your answer in **four explicit sections**: (1) Name the technique, (2) Define it in one sentence, (3) Justify the choice by linking to the design question, (4) Outline the build-test-learn steps. This structure maps directly to the KTU marking scheme and ensures full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT524 - INTERFACE DESIGN FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Input Devices: Sensors, Actuators, and Their Roles in CPS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)

In the context of **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)**, *input devices* constitute the physical-layer interface elements that mediate energy, matter, and information flow between the **physical process** and the **computational core**. They are broadly classified into two functional categories:

- **Sensors**: Transducing elements that convert a *physical stimulus* (temperature, pressure, displacement, light, etc.) into a measurable electrical (or optical/pneumatic) signal. They form the **Physical-to-Cyber (P2C)** bridge.
- **Actuators**: Transducing elements that convert a *control command* (electrical, hydraulic, or pneumatic signal) from the cyber core into a *physical action* (motion, force, heat, light). They form the **Cyber-to-Physical (C2P)** bridge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**: In CPS architecture, sensors and actuators are not standalone components — they are **distributed, networked, and time-synchronized** entities forming part of the **Sense–Compute–Actuate (SCA) loop**, the fundamental feedback cycle of every CPS.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine the **human body** as a Cyber-Physical System:

| Human Body Part | CPS Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Eyes, ears, skin, tongue | **Sensors** | Detect light, sound, pressure, taste |
| Brain + spinal cord | **Computational Core** | Processes signals, decides |
| Hands, legs, vocal cords | **Actuators** | Performs physical actions |
| Nervous system | **Communication Bus** | Carries signals to/from brain |

When you touch a hot pan (sensor → skin), your brain processes it (compute), and your hand jerks away (actuator → muscle). This entire loop happens in **milliseconds** — and a CPS does the same with embedded electronics.

> [!NOTE]
> A CPS without sensors is "blind"; without actuators it is "paralysed". Both are **mandatory** for any closed-loop control or autonomous behaviour.

## 1.3 Key Standardized Metrics (Bolded for KTU Recall)

The following performance metrics are **standardized** across sensor and actuator datasheets and are routinely tested in KTU examinations:

- **Accuracy**: Closeness of the measured value to the true value (expressed as % of Full Scale, **%FS**).
- **Precision**: Repeatability of measurements under identical conditions.
- **Sensitivity (S)**: Ratio of output change to input change, $S = \Delta Y / \Delta X$.
- **Resolution**: Smallest detectable change in input, bounded by the ADC bit width.
- **Linearity**: Maximum deviation of the transfer characteristic from an ideal straight line (**%FS** or **% reading**).
- **Hysteresis**: Maximum difference in output for the same input approached from opposite directions.
- **Dynamic Range (DR)**: Ratio of the largest to the smallest measurable signal, often expressed in **dB**.
- **Response Time / Time Constant ($\tau$)**: Time taken to reach **63.2 %** of the final value for a first-order system.
- **Bandwidth**: Frequency range over which the device operates linearly (-3 dB cutoff).
- **Dead Band / Dead Zone**: Range of input over which the output remains unchanged.

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sensor Classification (Hierarchical)

Sensors can be classified along **three orthogonal axes**, all of which are KTU-frequently-tested:

### A. By Power Requirement
- **Active Sensors** (self-generating): Generate their own electrical signal — e.g., **thermocouple** (Seebeck effect), **piezoelectric sensor**, **photovoltaic cell**. No external excitation needed.
- **Passive Sensors** (modulating): Require external excitation (voltage/current supply) — e.g., **thermistor**, **RTD**, **strain gauge**, **LVDT**, **potentiometer**. They *modulate* the excitation.

### B. By Output Signal Type
- **Analog Sensors**: Output is continuous (voltage 0–10 V, current 4–20 mA, resistance).
- **Digital Sensors**: Output is discrete, often serial (I²C, SPI, UART) — e.g., **DHT22**, **MPU6050**.

### C. By Physical Principle
- **Resistive** (RTD, thermistor, strain gauge)
- **Capacitive** (capacitive humidity, proximity)
- **Inductive** (LVDT, eddy-current probe)
- **Piezoelectric** (force, accelerometer)
- **Semiconductor / MEMS** (pressure, accelerometer, gyroscope)
- **Optical** (photodiode, LDR, LiDAR)
- **Electrochemical** (pH, gas sensors)

## 2.2 Actuator Classification

Actuators are classified by the **energy domain** they employ to produce physical work:

### A. Electrical Actuators
- **DC Motors**: Continuous rotation; speed ∝ voltage.
- **Stepper Motors**: Discrete angular steps; open-loop positioning.
- **Servo Motors**: Closed-loop DC motor with encoder feedback.
- **Solenoids**: Linear electromagnetic push/pull action.
- **Piezoelectric Actuators**: Sub-micron precision, high force.

### B. Hydraulic Actuators
- Use incompressible fluid (oil); high force density, slow response.
- Applications: Heavy machinery, aircraft flight controls.

### C. Pneumatic Actuators
- Use compressed air; compliant, clean, low force; fast response.
- Applications: Robotics grippers, valve control in process industries.

### D. Thermal / Shape-Memory Actuators
- **Shape Memory Alloys (SMA — Nitinol)**: Contract when heated.
- **Thermal bimorph strips**: Bend with temperature change.

### E. Micro / MEMS Actuators
- Electrostatic, electromagnetic, or thermal micro-actuators for lab-on-chip systems.

## 2.3 The Sense–Compute–Actuate (SCA) Loop in CPS

The fundamental closed-loop of every CPS follows this canonical structure:

1. **Sensing Phase**: Sensors sample physical state $x_p(t)$ at sampling instants $t_k = kT_s$.
2. **Communication Phase**: Sensor data is transmitted (often over a real-time bus like CAN, EtherCAT, TSN).
3. **Computation Phase**: Cyber controller computes control action $u(t)$ via algorithm (PID, MPC, fuzzy, neural).
4. **Actuation Phase**: Actuator applies $u(t)$ to the physical process, altering its state.
5. **Feedback**: New sensor readings close the loop with a total latency $T_{loop} = T_{sense} + T_{comm} + T_{compute} + T_{actuate}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Insight**: For *hard real-time* CPS (e.g., anti-lock braking, pacemakers), the loop latency $T_{loop}$ **must be strictly bounded** by the deadline $D$, i.e., $T_{loop} \le D$. Failure to meet this is a **system failure**, not merely a delayed response.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Expression | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Sensitivity** | $S = \dfrac{\Delta Y}{\Delta X}$ | $\Delta Y$ = output change (V), $\Delta X$ = input change (°C, N, etc.) |
| 2 | **Span (Full-Scale Range)** | $\text{FS} = Y_{\max} - Y_{\min}$ | Same units as output |
| 3 | **Resolution of N-bit ADC** | $R = \dfrac{\text{FS}}{2^{N} - 1}$ | $N$ = ADC bits, $R$ in least-significant-bit units (LSB) |
| 4 | **First-Order Time Response** | $y(t) = Y_{\infty}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$ | $\tau = RC$ (thermal), $RC$ (electrical), $L/R$ (inductive) |
| 5 | **Strain Gauge (Wheatstone Bridge)** | $\dfrac{V_o}{V_{ex}} = \dfrac{R_1 R_3 - R_2 R_4}{(R_1+R_2)(R_3+R_4)}$ | $V_{ex}$ = excitation, $V_o$ = bridge output |
| 6 | **Gauge Factor** | $GF = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$ | $\varepsilon$ = strain (dimensionless) |
| 7 | **Thermocouple Seebeck Voltage** | $V = S_{AB} \cdot \Delta T$ | $S_{AB}$ = Seebeck coefficient (μV/°C) |
| 8 | **RTD Resistance** | $R_T = R_0 \left[1 + \alpha(T - T_0)\right]$ | $\alpha$ = temperature coefficient (≈ 0.00385 for Pt) |
| 9 | **Dynamic Range in dB** | $DR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{Y_{\max}}{Y_{\min}}\right)$ | For voltage-domain signals |
| 10 | **DC Motor Torque-Speed** | $\omega = \omega_{NL} - k_T \cdot \tau$ | $\omega_{NL}$ = no-load speed, $k_T$ = torque constant |
| 11 | **Piezo Charge Output** | $Q = d \cdot F$ | $d$ = piezoelectric charge coefficient (C/N) |
| 12 | **Actuator Power Density** | $P_d = \dfrac{P_{\max}}{m_{\text{actuator}}}$ | Used for actuator selection in robotics |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive CPS** (Engine Control Unit): Crankshaft position sensor (Hall-effect) + oxygen sensor (lambda probe) + fuel injector actuator — closed-loop fuel-air control.
- **Healthcare CPS** (Insulin Pump): Glucose sensor (electrochemical) + micropump actuator — closed-loop diabetes management.
- **Industrial IoT CPS** (Smart Factory): MEMS accelerometer on spindle + servo motor actuator — predictive maintenance loop.
- **Aerospace CPS** (Fly-by-Wire): LVDT position sensor on control surface + hydraulic actuator — flight control loop with bounded latency.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Resolution of an N-bit ADC Interfaced to a Sensor

**Problem Statement**: A pressure sensor has a full-scale output of **0–10 V** mapped to a pressure range of **0–100 kPa**. The sensor signal is digitized by a **12-bit ADC**. Find (a) the LSB resolution in volts, (b) the per-bit pressure resolution, and (c) the smallest detectable pressure change.

### Step 1 — Identify the Full-Scale Voltage Range
The sensor output voltage range corresponds to the ADC input range:
$$V_{FS} = V_{\max} - V_{\min} = 10\text{ V} - 0\text{ V} = 10\text{ V}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Number of Quantization Levels
For an N-bit ADC, the total number of discrete output codes is $2^N$:
$$L_{total} = 2^{12} = 4096\text{ levels}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the KTU Resolution Formula
Each Least Significant Bit (LSB) corresponds to:
$$R_{volt} = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{N} - 1} = \dfrac{10}{4096 - 1} = \dfrac{10}{4095}$$

$$R_{volt} \approx 2.442 \times 10^{-3}\text{ V} \approx 2.44\text{ mV/LSB}$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Pressure Resolution
The sensor transfer gain is linear:
$$K_{sensor} = \dfrac{P_{\max} - P_{\min}}{V_{\max} - V_{\min}} = \dfrac{100\text{ kPa} - 0}{10\text{ V} - 0} = 10\text{ kPa/V}$$

Therefore, per-bit pressure resolution:
$$R_{pressure} = R_{volt} \times K_{sensor} = 2.442 \times 10^{-3} \times 10 = 2.442 \times 10^{-2}\text{ kPa/LSB}$$

$$R_{pressure} \approx 24.42\text{ Pa/LSB}$$

### Step 5 — Interpretation
The smallest detectable pressure change (pressure resolution) is **≈ 24.42 Pa**, which is sufficient for most industrial pneumatic monitoring CPS applications.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — First-Order Step Response of a Thermal Sensor

**Problem**: A thermistor with thermal time constant $\tau = 5\text{ s}$ is suddenly immersed in a water bath at $T_{\infty} = 80^\circ$C, starting from $T_0 = 20^\circ$C. Compute the temperature reading at $t = 3\text{ s}$.

### Step 1 — Establish the Governing ODE
Newton's law of cooling for a lumped thermal mass:
$$\tau \dfrac{dT}{dt} + T(t) = T_{\infty}$$

### Step 2 — Solve Using Integrating Factor $e^{t/\tau}$
$$\dfrac{d}{dt}\!\left[T(t) e^{t/\tau}\right] = \dfrac{T_{\infty}}{\tau} e^{t/\tau}$$

Integrating both sides:
$$T(t) e^{t/\tau} = T_{\infty} e^{t/\tau} + C$$

### Step 3 — Apply Initial Condition $T(0) = T_0$
$$T_0 \cdot 1 = T_{\infty} \cdot 1 + C \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; C = T_0 - T_{\infty}$$

### Step 4 — Final Closed-Form Solution
$$T(t) = T_{\infty} + (T_0 - T_{\infty})e^{-t/\tau}$$

Substitute $T_0 = 20^\circ$C, $T_{\infty} = 80^\circ$C, $t = 3\text{ s}$, $\tau = 5\text{ s}$:
$$T(3) = 80 + (20 - 80)e^{-3/5} = 80 - 60 \cdot e^{-0.6}$$

Computing the exponential:
$$e^{-0.6} \approx 0.5488$$

Therefore:
$$T(3) = 80 - 60 \times 0.5488 = 80 - 32.928 = 47.07^\circ\text{C}$$

### Step 5 — Physical Interpretation
At $t = 3\text{ s}$, the thermistor has reached only $(47.07 - 20)/(80 - 20) \times 100\% \approx 45.1\%$ of the total temperature swing — confirming the slow thermal transient characteristic of first-order thermal sensors.

---

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap
