p = 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{90/20} = 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{4.5}
$$

$$
p = 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 31622.77 = 0.632 \ \text{Pa}
$$

**Step 3 — Sanity check:** At **120 dB SPL**, $p \approx 20$ Pa (the threshold of pain). Our 90 dB alarm is at **6.32 × 10⁻¹ Pa**, which is loud but safe for an 8-hour shift.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Fitts's Law for Haptic Target Acquisition

When a user is asked to acquire a haptic target (e.g., select a button on a touchscreen with confirmation vibration), **Fitts's Law** predicts the time required:

$$
MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)
$$

where $D$ is distance to target, $W$ is target width, and $a, b$ are empirically fit constants (for hand movements, $a \approx 50$ ms, $b \approx 150$ ms/bit).

**Numerical example**: A surgeon selects a tool on a touchscreen. Distance $D = 0.10$ m, width $W = 0.02$ m. Compute the index of difficulty (ID) and movement time.

**Step 1 — Index of Difficulty:**

$$
ID = \log_2\!\left(\frac{0.10}{0.02} + 1\right) = \log_2(5 + 1) = \log_2(6) \approx 2.585 \ \text{bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Movement time:**

$$
MT = 50 + 150 \times 2.585 = 50 + 387.7 = 437.7 \ \text{ms}
$$

**Design insight**: To bring $MT$ below **300 ms**, the designer must either reduce $D$ (rearrange UI) or increase $W$ (larger button).

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Reverberation Time for CPS Control Rooms

A CPS control room (e.g., power-plant SCADA) requires controlled acoustics so that auditory alarms are intelligible. The **Sabine equation** gives reverberation time:

$$
T_{60} = \frac{0.161 \cdot V}{A}
$$

where $V$ is room volume (m³) and $A$ is total absorption (Sabins).

**Numerical example**: A control room is $10 \times 8 \times 3$ m. Walls have absorption $\alpha = 0.3$ over an area $S = 2(10 \times 8 + 10 \times 3 + 8 \times 3) = 2(80 + 30 + 24) = 268 \ \text{m}^2$. Compute $T_{60}$.

**Step 1 — Volume:**

$$
V = 10 \times 8 \times 3 = 240 \ \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Total absorption:**

$$
A = S \alpha = 268 \times 0.3 = 80.4 \ \text{Sabins}
$$

**Step 3 — Reverberation time:**

$$
T_{60} = \frac{0.161 \times 240}{80.4} = \frac{38.64}{80.4} = 0.481 \ \text{s}
$$

**Design verdict**: $T_{60} \approx 0.5$ s is within the **IEC 61260** recommended range (0.4–0.6 s) for intelligibility.

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Haptic vs. Auditory

| **Criterion** | **Haptic Feedback** | **Auditory Feedback** |
|---|---|---|
| Sensory latency | **30–50 ms** | **120–180 ms** |
| Spatial resolution | High (2–4 mm tactile) | Low (sound source localization ±10°) |
| Privacy | **Excellent** (silent to others) | **Poor** (audible to all) |
| Eyes-free / Hands-free | Hands-free, but device contact required | Both eyes-free and hands-free |
| Alarm urgency perception | Moderate | **Very high** (startle reflex) |
| Suitable environments | Noisy (factories) | Quiet (hospitals, control rooms) |
| Cognitive load | Low | Medium (multiple beeps may confuse) |
| Failure mode | Actuator burnout, calibration drift | Speaker damage, masking by ambient noise |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Haptic Feedback System in a CPS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical Process] --> B[Sensor: Force, Torque, Position]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioning and ADC]
    C --> D[Haptic Controller DSP]
    D --> E[Haptic Actuator: LRA / Voice Coil / ERM]
    E --> F[Human Skin and Mechanoreceptors]
    F --> G[Cognitive Interpretation by Operator]
    G --> A
    H[Safety Watchdog] -.-> D
    I[Impedance Control Law] -.-> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**: Data flows clockwise. The operator closes the loop by adjusting their action in response to the felt stimulus. The dashed lines represent *control parameters* injected into the DSP (e.g., the impedance law $F = -B v - K x$). The Safety Watchdog is a KTU-mandated redundancy that disables the actuator if $L_{total} > 100$ ms.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Auditory Feedback System in a CPS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[CPS Variable: Temp, Distance, State] --> B1[Mapping Function: Sonification Rule]
    B1 --> C1[Audio Synthesis Engine: DSP or Codec]
    C1 --> D1[DAC and Amplifier]
    D1 --> E1[Acoustic Transducer: Speaker, Buzzer, Bone Conduction]
    E1 --> F1[Human Ear: Outer, Middle, Inner]
    F1 --> G1[Auditory Cortex Interpretation]
    G1 --> H1[Operator Decision]
    H1 --> A1
    J1[Alarm Priority Manager] -.-> C1
    K1[Ambient Noise Sensor] -.-> C1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Design Note**: The *Alarm Priority Manager* ensures that critical alarms override lower-priority beeps, and the *Ambient Noise Sensor* dynamically adjusts the alarm loudness to maintain the 15 dB SNR margin (ISO 7731).

## 4.3 Decision Flow — Choosing Between Haptic and Auditory Feedback

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([CPS Event to Communicate]) --> Q1{Is visual channel overloaded?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is the operator mobile or hands-busy?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Is the environment noisy?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Haptic[Use Haptic Feedback]
    Q3 -- No --> Multi[Use Multimodal Haptic + Auditory]
    Q2 -- No --> Auditory[Use Auditory Feedback]
    Q1 -- No --> Visual[Visual feedback is sufficient]
    Haptic --> End([Communicate Event])
    Auditory --> End
    Multi --> End
    Visual --> End
```

## 4.4 Design Consideration Wheel — CPS Feedback Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DC[Design Consideration] --> S1[Safety: Fail-Silent Actuator]
    DC --> S2[Latency: sub 30 ms Haptic / sub 100 ms Audio]
    DC --> S3[Usability: Matches Operator Mental Model]
    DC --> S4[Accessibility: Supports Impaired Users]
    DC --> S5[Robustness: Works Under Stress, Noise, Fatigue]
    DC --> S6[Standards: ISO 7731, IEC 61508, ISO 26262]
    S1 --> Outcome[Compliant CPS Interface]
    S2 --> Outcome
    S3 --> Outcome
    S4 --> Outcome
    S5 --> Outcome
    S6 --> Outcome
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`
**Differentiate between tactile and kinesthetic haptic feedback. Give one example of each in a CPS application.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — KTU marking scheme)**:
- **Tactile haptic feedback** stimulates the skin's mechanoreceptors (Meissner, Merkel, Ruffini, Pacinian corpuscles) to produce sensations like vibration, pressure, or texture. It is sensed superficially.
  - *Example*: A smartphone's vibration motor (LRA/ERM) that pulses when a CPS alert is received.
- **Kinesthetic haptic feedback** stimulates muscles, tendons, and joint receptors to convey information about force, weight, or position of a remote or virtual object. It is sensed deep in the body.
  - *Example*: A teleoperated robotic arm in a hazardous environment (e.g., nuclear cleanup) that lets the operator feel the resistance of the gripped object.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall**: Students often write *vibration* as a generic example for both. Marks are awarded only if you clearly distinguish **skin-level (cutaneous)** vs **muscle/joint-level (kinesthetic)** receptors.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any three design considerations for auditory feedback in a CPS safety-critical interface.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — KTU marking scheme)**:
1. **Loudness margin** — The alarm must be at least **15 dB above ambient noise** (ISO 7731) to be detected reliably.
2. **Pitch and frequency spacing** — Distinct alarms should differ in frequency by at least the **Just-Noticeable Difference (JND)** of ~0.3% (about 3 Hz at 1 kHz) to avoid confusion.
3. **Alarm priority hierarchy** — Critical alarms must override non-critical ones; use unique timbres (e.g., warbling for danger, single tone for caution) to communicate urgency.
4. *(Acceptable 4th)* **Localization** — In multi-operator rooms, the alarm should be spatially identifiable using **3D audio** or **directional speakers**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall**: Writing "alarm should be loud" alone scores 0.5 marks; the **15 dB margin** is the KTU-recommended quantification.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**"Haptic feedback is a critical non-visual channel in Cyber-Physical Systems."**
**(a)** Explain the closed-loop architecture of a haptic feedback system in a CPS, with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of impedance control in rendering virtual stiffness. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A haptic device is designed for teleoperated surgery. The force sensor has a bandwidth of 300 Hz. Compute the minimum sampling rate, the total loop period, and the per-stage latency if the system imposes a 30 ms total latency budget divided equally among sensing, control, and actuation. Justify why haptic feedback in surgery must remain below this latency. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Block architecture (3 marks):**
A haptic CPS contains six functional blocks in a closed loop:
1. **Physical process** (e.g., tissue being cut).
2. **Sensor** (force/torque transducer, encoder, or IMU).
3. **Signal conditioning** (amplification, low-pass filtering, ADC).
4. **Controller** (DSP running the control law).
5. **Actuator** (voice coil, LRA, or pneumatic muscle).
6. **Human operator** (mechanoreceptors + motor response).

**Step 2 — Role of impedance control (3 marks):**
Impedance control maps the *position error* $x_e$ between the master handle and the virtual slave into a *force* $F_{rendered}$ delivered to the operator:

$$
F_{rendered} = -K \cdot x_e - B \cdot \dot{x}_e
$$

- $K$ is **virtual stiffness** — a higher $K$ makes virtual objects feel "hard."
- $B$ is **virtual damping** — a higher $B$ makes the object feel "slimy" or viscous.
- By adjusting $K$ and $B$ in real time, the CPS can simulate contact with a rigid bone, a soft tissue, or a slippery membrane.

**Step 3 — Closing remark (1 mark):**
In surgical CPS, this means the surgeon *feels* the difference between cutting through fat versus muscle, *without* a visual cue.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given**: $B_h = 300$ Hz, $L_{total} = 30$ ms, 3 equal stages.

**Step 1 — Apply safety factor 4× to compute minimum sampling rate (2 marks):**

$$
f_s = 4 \times B_h = 4 \times 300 = 1200 \ \text{Hz} = 1.2 \ \text{kHz}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute loop period (1 mark):**

$$
T_{loop} = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{1200} = 8.33 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{s} \approx 0.833 \ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Per-stage latency (2 marks):**

$$
L_{sens} = L_{ctrl} = L_{act} = \frac{30}{3} = 10 \ \text{ms each}
$$

**Step 4 — Justification (2 marks):**
Human haptic perception can detect delays above **30 ms**. In teleoperated surgery, a delay above this threshold causes:
- **Loss of situational awareness** — the surgeon cuts before feeling resistance.
- **Oscillatory instability** — the closed loop becomes unstable, leading to overshoot and potential tissue damage.
- **Compensatory slowdown** — the surgeon must pause frequently, extending procedure time and increasing patient risk.

Therefore, a **≤ 30 ms** end-to-end latency is a hard constraint in surgical CPS.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks, Alternative) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**"Auditory feedback enables eyes-free monitoring of CPS processes."**
**(a)** With a suitable block diagram, describe the closed-loop architecture of an auditory feedback system. Explain the concept of *earcons* and their design guidelines. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A SCADA control room for a thermal power plant has an ambient noise of **78 dB SPL**. Compute the required alarm SPL per ISO 7731, and the absolute sound pressure in Pascals. If the room is **12 m × 10 m × 4 m** with average wall absorption coefficient **0.35**, compute the reverberation time $T_{60}$ and comment on whether the room is suitable for alarm intelligibility. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Block architecture (3 marks):**
The auditory CPS feedback chain:
1. **CPS variable** (e.g., reactor temperature, valve state).
2. **Mapping function** (sonification rule — pitch ∝ temperature, beep rate ∝ distance).
3. **Audio synthesis engine** (DSP generates waveform).
4. **DAC and amplifier** (digital-to-analog conversion + power amp).
5. **Acoustic transducer** (speaker, buzzer, or bone-conduction driver).
6. **Human auditory system** (outer/middle/inner ear → cochlea → auditory nerve).
7. **Operator cognition and action** (e.g., reduce fuel flow).

**Step 2 — Earcons and design guidelines (3 marks):**
An **earcon** is a short, structured, abstract sound representing a CPS event. Per **Blattner-Greenberg-Sumikawa (1989)**:
- **Nominal earcons** — single tones, used for confirmation (e.g., "task complete").
- **Source earcons** — mimic real-world sounds (e.g., a "click" for switch activation).
- **Family earcons** — share a base timbre within a category (e.g., all "warning" alarms use a warbling tone).
- **Musical earcons** — use pitch intervals (major third for safe, minor second for danger) to convey meaning.

Design rules: (i) keep duration **< 500 ms** for alarms, (ii) ensure pitch JND ≥ 3% separation, (iii) use a unique timbre for each priority level.

**Step 3 — Closing remark (1 mark):**
Earcons are *learned* symbols; the operator must be trained, unlike auditory icons which are *intuitive*.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given**: $L_{p,amb} = 78$ dB SPL, $V = 12 \times 10 \times 4 = 480$ m³, $\alpha = 0.35$.

**Step 1 — Required alarm SPL (2 marks):**

$$
L_{p,alarm} = L_{p,amb} + 15 = 78 + 15 = 93 \ \text{dB SPL}
$$

**Step 2 — Absolute sound pressure (2 marks):**

$$
p = p_0 \cdot 10^{L_p / 20} = 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{93/20}
$$

$$
p = 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{4.65} = 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 44668 = 0.893 \ \text{Pa}
$$

**Step 3 — Total surface area of the room (1 mark):**

$$
S = 2(LW + LH + WH) = 2(12 \times 10 + 12 \times 4 + 10 \times 4) = 2(120 + 48 + 40) = 416 \ \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Total absorption (1 mark):**

$$
A = S \alpha = 416 \times 0.35 = 145.6 \ \text{Sabins}
$$

**Step 5 — Reverberation time (1 mark):**

$$
T_{60} = \frac{0.161 \cdot V}{A} = \frac{0.161 \times 480}{145.6} = \frac{77.28}{145.6} = 0.531 \ \text{s}
$$

**Step 6 — Verdict (any valid comment, treated as bonus 0 marks — built into the 7-mark budget):**
$T_{60} \approx 0.53$ s falls within the **IEC 61260** recommended range of **0.4–0.6 s** for alarm-intelligible control rooms. Hence, the room is suitable.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls (per KTU evaluator feedback)**:
> 1. Forgetting the **15 dB margin** in Step 1 — costs 1 full mark.
> 2. Using $p_0 = 20$ Pa instead of $20 \ \mu\text{Pa}$ — silently wrong answer.
> 3. Omitting the area factor of 2 in Step 3 — halves the result, loses 1 mark.
> 4. Stating the answer without units — KTU deducts 0.5 marks per quantity.

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section before every exam.**

- ✅ **Haptic** = touch-based; **Auditory** = sound-based. Both are *non-visual* feedback channels.
- ✅ Haptic has **two sub-types**: tactile (skin) and kinesthetic (muscle/joint).
- ✅ Auditory has **two sub-types**: discrete (beeps/alarms) and continuous (sonification).
- ✅ Haptic latency must be **≤ 30 ms**; auditory alarm latency must be **≤ 100 ms**.
- ✅ Haptic sampling rate: **1 kHz** typical (4× the tactile bandwidth, e.g., 250 Hz).
- ✅ Impedance control force: $F_{rendered} = -K \Delta x - B \Delta v$.
- ✅ Sound pressure level: $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p / p_0)$, with $p_0 = 20 \ \mu\text{Pa}$.
- ✅ **15 dB margin** over ambient noise is the ISO 7731 rule for alarms.
- ✅ Pitch JND ≈ **0.3%** (3 Hz at 1 kHz) — minimum spacing between earcons.
- ✅ Fitts's Law: $MT = a + b \log_2(D/W + 1)$ — predicts haptic-target acquisition time.
- ✅ Sabine equation: $T_{60} = 0.161 \cdot V / A$ — control room acoustic design.
- ✅ Receptors to remember: Meissner (light touch), Merkel (pressure), Ruffini (stretch), Pacinian (vibration).
- ✅ Standards: **ISO 7731** (auditory alarms), **IEC 61508** (industrial CPS safety), **ISO 26262** (automotive).
- ✅ **Earcons** are learned; **auditory icons** are intuitive. Choose by operator training level.
- ✅ **Impedance control** measures position, outputs force; **admittance control** measures force, outputs position.
- ✅ Haptic feedback is **silent** (privacy-preserving) and ideal for **noisy** environments.
- ✅ Auditory feedback is **omnidirectional** and ideal for **critical alarms** due to the startle reflex.
- ✅ Always cite a **standard** in your exam answer to score full marks in design questions.
- ✅ For numericals, **always include units** (dB SPL, Hz, ms, N, m, m³, Pa) — KTU deducts 0.5 per missing unit.
- ✅ In tables, **never use the pipe character** `|` for absolute value — use $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ in LaTeX.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interface Models and Information Visualization: Information Visualization Techniques for CPS Interfaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT524 - INTERFACE DESIGN FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Interface Models and Information Visualization: Information Visualization Techniques for CPS Interfaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Information Visualization (InfoVis)** is the graphical representation of abstract, multidimensional data harvested from Cyber-Physical System (CPS) sensor networks, actuators, and computational cores, designed to amplify human cognition, accelerate situational awareness, and support decision-making across supervisory, diagnostic, and predictive operational layers.

Within the **CPS interface paradigm**, information visualization operates as the *semantic bridge* between the cyber layer (computation, communication, control logic) and the human operator (the cognitive endpoint). It transforms raw telemetry into perceptually optimized visual encodings that respect the bandwidth and resolution limits of human visual perception.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** Information Visualization in CPS is *not* merely "showing graphs on a screen." It is a **structured cognitive engineering discipline** that combines perceptual psychology, data semantics, real-time systems, and interaction design to translate cyber-physical phenomena into actionable visual artifacts.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Cockpit Analogy

Imagine the cockpit of a modern commercial airliner (e.g., a Boeing 787). The pilots are surrounded by **six large LCD screens** displaying airspeed, altitude, attitude, engine health, fuel, navigation routes, weather radar, and synthetic vision of terrain. None of this information is "raw wire data" — every parameter has been **digitized, filtered, prioritized, and visually encoded** so the pilot can grasp a 1,500-parameter system in a single glance.

Now scale that analogy to a CPS:
- The **aircraft** = your CPS (e.g., smart grid, autonomous vehicle, smart factory).
- The **sensors** in the wings/fuselage = CPS sensor nodes.
- The **flight computers** = cyber controllers.
- The **LCD displays** = your **information visualization interface**.
- The **pilot** = the human operator / supervisor.

The job of an InfoVis designer is to ensure the operator never feels "flying blind," even when the CPS contains thousands of distributed variables updating at millisecond rates.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** In CPS interfaces, visualization must be **real-time, fault-tolerant, and perceptually faithful**. A lag of 200 ms or a misleading color mapping can have kinetic consequences (literally — autonomous vehicle crashes, surgical robot errors).

---

## 1.3 Why Visualization is Non-Negotiable in CPS

Human cognition is bounded. The classic estimation often cited is that the human working memory can hold **7 ± 2 chunks of information** at a time (Miller's Law). CPS can stream **millions of data points per second**. Without visualization:

1. Data is **invisible** to the human decision-maker.
2. Patterns (anomalies, trends, correlations) are buried in raw values.
3. Latency between event and comprehension becomes unacceptable.
4. Trust between human and autonomous system erodes (*opacity problem*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Visualization Bandwidth Metrics (Industry Reference):**
> - **Pre-attentive processing threshold:** ~200 ms (anything processed faster than this is "pre-attentive," e.g., color, motion).
> - **Human visual acuity:** ~60 pixels per degree (relevant for dashboard pixel-density design).
> - **Refresh rate requirement for real-time CPS:** ≥ **30 Hz** minimum, **60 Hz** preferred for supervisory control.
> - **Foveal vs peripheral vision split:** Foveal covers only ~**2° of visual angle** with high acuity.

---

## 1.4 The Three-Layer Visualization Stack in CPS

| Layer | Function | Example in CPS |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Layer** | Acquisition, fusion, storage | Sensor stream, time-series DB |
| **Mapping Layer** | Translation of data → visual variables | Color map, glyph, layout |
| **Presentation Layer** | Final rendering on HMI / AR / mobile | Dashboard, HUD, tablet |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Visualization Pipeline (CPS-Specific)

The canonical CPS visualization pipeline consists of **seven ordered stages**. Each stage must be designed explicitly; failing at any one corrupts the entire information chain.

1. **Data Acquisition** — Raw sensor samples arrive at sampling rate $f_s$ (Hz). The Nyquist constraint requires $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ where $f_{max}$ is the highest signal frequency of interest.

2. **Preprocessing & Cleansing** — Noise filtering (e.g., Kalman filter, moving average), outlier rejection, unit normalization. Missing data imputation may use linear interpolation or model-based estimation.

3. **Data Transformation & Aggregation** — Dimensionality reduction (PCA, t-SNE for high-dimensional CPS state spaces). Time-window aggregation (e.g., 1-second rolling mean of vibration data).

4. **Visual Mapping (the Core of InfoVis)** — Mapping data attributes to **visual variables**. Bertin's seven retinal variables (revisited by Card, Mackinlay):
   - Position (x, y)
   - Size
   - Shape
   - Value (lightness)
   - Color (hue)
   - Orientation
   - Texture (motion is often added as an 8th)

5. **Rendering & Layout** — Algorithms to place marks on screen without occlusion. Examples: force-directed (network CPS), treemap (hierarchical resource use), geographic projection (spatial CPS).

6. **Interaction Layer** — Selection, brushing, linking, zoom, filter, details-on-demand (Shneiderman's mantra: *"Overview first, zoom and filter, then details on demand"*).

7. **Display & Cognitive Feedback Loop** — Rendering on physical/optical hardware. Operator perceives → acts → CPS responds → visualization updates.

> [!NOTE]
> **Shneiderman's Mantra (1996)** — *"Overview first, zoom and filter, then details on demand."* This is **mandatory reading** for any KTU question on visualization design principles.

---

## 2.2 Taxonomy of Information Visualization Techniques for CPS

### A. **Time-Series Visualizations**
The workhorse of CPS dashboards (SCADA, process control, IoT).
- **Line charts** — for continuous signals (temperature, pressure).
- **Sparklines** — dense, word-sized trend indicators.
- **Stacked area charts** — cumulative resource use (CPU, memory, network).
- **Gantt charts** — task scheduling in industrial CPS.

### B. **Geospatial / Spatial Visualizations**
- **Choropleth maps** — color-encoded regions (e.g., smart grid load by district).
- **Heatmaps** — 2D density/intensity overlays (traffic, pollution, sensor coverage).
- **3D terrain with overlaid glyphs** — UAV swarm telemetry over a city.
- **Isarithmic maps** — contour lines for continuous fields (radiation, magnetic flux).

### C. **Network / Graph Visualizations**
- **Node-link diagrams** — for CPS communication topology, sensor networks, attack graphs.
- **Adjacency matrices** — for dense graphs.
- **Arc diagrams** — for sequential or hierarchical dependencies.

### D. **Multivariate / High-Dimensional Visualizations**
- **Parallel coordinates** — for N-dimensional CPS state vectors.
- **Scatterplot matrices (SPLOM)** — pairwise correlations.
- **Radial/coxcomb charts** — multi-attribute dashboards.
- **Dimensionality-reduced projections** — PCA, t-SNE, UMAP for ML-based anomaly clusters.

### E. **Hierarchical / Tree Visualizations**
- **Treemaps** — proportional nesting (memory map of embedded CPS controller).
- **Sunburst diagrams** — radial hierarchy.
- **Indented trees** — classic drill-down for fault trees.

### F. **3D, Immersive & AR/VR Visualizations**
- **Digital twin overlays** — exact 3D replica of the physical CPS with live data injection.
- **AR head-up displays** — annotations projected on operator's view of real equipment.
- **VR control rooms** — multi-screen immersive cockpits for industrial CPS.
- **Point clouds and LiDAR visualization** — autonomous vehicle perception.

### G. **Statistical / Distributional Visualizations**
- **Box plots, violin plots** — distribution of CPS sensor noise.
- **Histograms** — frequency of alarm severities.
- **Q-Q plots** — distribution fitting for fault diagnostics.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

| Concept | Formula / Principle | Engineering Use in CPS |
|---|---|---|
| **Nyquist Sampling** | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | Ensures no aliasing in visualized sensor data |
| **Frame Budget (Real-Time)** | $T_{frame} = \frac{1}{FPS} \leq T_{deadline}$ | Guarantees visualization updates within control loop deadline |
| **Pre-attentive Limit** | $\Delta t \leq 200 \text{ ms}$ | Visual cues processed without focused attention |
| **Visual Acuity (pixels/degree)** | $\theta_{pixel} = \frac{1}{60}^\circ$ | Determines minimum readable text/glyph size |
| **Miller's Working Memory** | $N_{chunks} \approx 7 \pm 2$ | Max simultaneous visual elements per view |
| **Data-Ink Ratio (Tufte)** | $R_{ink} = \frac{\text{data ink}}{\text{total ink}}$ | Measures visualization efficiency |
| **Color Distance (CIEDE2000)** | $\Delta E_{00}$ | Perceptual color difference; $\Delta E_{00} > 5$ is clearly distinguishable |
| **Stevens' Power Law (perception)** | $\psi = k \cdot S^{n}$ | Maps stimulus magnitude to perceived intensity (length $n=1$, area $n=0.7$, brightness $n=0.33$) |
| **Fitts's Law (interaction)** | $T = a + b \cdot \log_2\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)$ | Time to acquire visual target; guides button sizing on HMI |
| **Gestalt Principles** | Proximity, Similarity, Continuity, Closure, Common Region | Guide perceptual grouping of visualization marks |
| **Perceptual Scalability** | Marks visible until crowding point $\sim 10^4$ pixels | Limits of dense CPS displays |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Tip:** For Part B 14-mark questions, **always quote** Stevens' Power Law (for appropriate visual variables) and Fitts's Law (for interaction design) when justifying design choices. They are **high-yield** in CPS interface papers.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Smart Manufacturing (Industry 4.0):** Operators use multivariate dashboards to oversee 50+ KPIs on a single screen.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Bird's-eye-view + sensor-fused AR HUDs visualize perception, planning, and prediction simultaneously.
- **Smart Grids:** Geospatial heatmaps show load distribution; time-series charts show demand-response cycles.
- **Medical CPS (Surgical Robots):** Vital signs, force feedback, and 3D anatomical overlays merge in immersive visualization.
- **Smart Cities:** Sensor overlays on city maps show traffic, pollution, noise, and energy in unified AR layers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Trade-off Principle:** *No visualization is universally "best."* The choice is always a function of: data type (1D/2D/3D/N-D), data size, user task (monitor / analyze / explore / present), and update rate. KTU examiners frequently test this **task–visualization matching** principle.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Sampling Theorem Application for CPS Visualization

**Problem Statement:** A CPS vibration sensor monitors a motor with maximum fault-induced frequency $f_{max} = 2.5 \text{ kHz}$. The visualization dashboard must display the waveform accurately. Determine the minimum sampling rate and the resulting frame-update budget if the dashboard renders at 60 FPS.

**Step 1 — Identify the maximum signal frequency.**

$$f_{max} = 2.5 \text{ kHz} = 2500 \text{ Hz}$$

This is the highest meaningful frequency component in the motor's vibration signature (bearing defect characteristic frequency).

**Step 2 — Apply the Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem.**

$$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the value.**

$$f_s \geq 2 \cdot 2500 = 5000 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 4 — Account for engineering safety margin (typically 2.5× to 5× Nyquist).**

$$f_s^{design} = k \cdot f_{Nyquist} = 5 \cdot 5000 = 25000 \text{ Hz}$$

Here $k = 5$ is the engineering oversampling factor. So we sample at **25 kHz**.

**Step 5 — Compute the frame-update period.**

$$T_{frame} = \frac{1}{FPS} = \frac{1}{60} \approx 0.01667 \text{ s} \approx 16.67 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the samples per displayed frame.**

$$N_{samples/frame} = f_s \cdot T_{frame} = 25000 \cdot 0.01667 \approx 416.7$$

So each frame must display **~417 samples** per sensor channel — achievable but justifies downsampling or windowed aggregation for multiple channels.

**Step 7 — Conclusion.** Visualization must downsample or aggregate (e.g., envelope extraction, RMS) to fit perceptual rendering budget.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Fitts's Law Applied to CPS Emergency Stop Button

**Problem Statement:** An industrial CPS control panel places an emergency stop button at distance $D = 30 \text{ cm}$ from the operator's typical hand rest. The button is circular with diameter $W = 4 \text{ cm}$. Empirical constants: $a = 50 \text{ ms}$, $b = 150 \text{ ms/bit}$. Compute the acquisition time.

**Step 1 — Recall Fitts's Law.**

$$T = a + b \cdot \log_2\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)$$

**Step 2 — Compute the index of difficulty (ID).**

$$ID = \log_2\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right) = \log_2\left(\frac{30}{4} + 1\right)$$

$$\frac{D}{W} = \frac{30}{4} = 7.5$$

$$ID = \log_2(7.5 + 1) = \log_2(8.5)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the logarithm.**

$$\log_2(8.5) = \frac{\ln(8.5)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{2.1401}{0.6931} \approx 3.087 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the movement time.**

$$T = 50 + 150 \cdot 3.087$$

$$T = 50 + 463.05 \approx 513 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret.**

A movement time of ~513 ms is acceptable for non-emergency controls but **borderline for true emergencies**. Hence, real industrial CPS use **larger E-stop buttons** (often $W = 6 \text{ cm}$) or **proximity sensors** to reduce $D$ to near zero, drastically cutting $T$.

**Step 6 — Redesign check.** If $W$ increases to $6 \text{ cm}$:

$$ID_{new} = \log_2\left(\frac{30}{6} + 1\right) = \log_2(6) \approx 2.585 \text{ bits}$$

$$T_{new} = 50 + 150 \cdot 2.585 = 50 + 387.7 = 437.7 \text{ ms}$$

A **15% reduction** in critical response time.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Color Mapping Using CIEDE2000 Threshold

**Problem Statement:** A CPS temperature heatmap uses a sequential colormap. The designer wants adjacent temperature bands to be unambiguously distinguishable. Verify whether a hue shift from yellow ($\text{RGB}_{yellow} = (255, 255, 0)$) to orange ($\text{RGB}_{orange} = (255, 165, 0)$) meets the perceptual threshold $\Delta E_{00} \geq 5$.

**Step 1 — Convert RGB to CIELAB (computational, here we give pre-computed values).**

Yellow in CIELAB: $L^* = 97.14$, $a^* = -21.55$, $b^* = 94.48$
Orange in CIELAB: $L^* = 75.13$, $a^* = 25.74$, $b^* = 78.41$

**Step 2 — Compute differences.**

$$\Delta L^* = 75.13 - 97.14 = -22.01$$

$$\Delta a^* = 25.74 - (-21.55) = 47.29$$

$$\Delta b^* = 78.41 - 94.48 = -16.07$$

**Step 3 — Compute CIEDE2000.** (Full formula omitted for brevity, but using standard reference implementation):

$$\Delta E_{00} \approx 28.5$$

**Step 4 — Compare to threshold.**

$$\Delta E_{00} = 28.5 \gg 5$$

The color pair is **strongly distinguishable** — acceptable for alarm gradations.

**Step 5 — Conclusion.** For critical alarms, choose color pairs with $\Delta E_{00} \geq 10$. For decorative non-critical zones, $\Delta E_{00} \geq 5$ is acceptable.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing Visualization Type

| CPS Task | Best Visualization | Why | Alternative |
|---|---|---|---|
| Monitor 1 process variable over time | **Line chart** | Continuous, easy trend reading | Sparkline (if space-constrained) |
| Monitor 1 process variable over time (alarm-focused) | **Threshold line + color band** | Pre-attentive alarm detection | Bullet chart |
| Compare 10+ variables over time | **Small multiples / line grid** | Avoids occlusion, supports comparison | Parallel coordinates |
| Show correlation between 2 variables | **Scatterplot** | Direct relational view | 2D histogram |
| Show correlation across N variables | **Scatterplot matrix (SPLOM)** | Pairwise complete | Parallel coordinates |
| Show network/communication topology | **Node-link diagram** | Familiar topology metaphor | Adjacency matrix (if dense) |
| Show hierarchy (fault tree, system decomposition) | **Treemap** | Space-efficient, proportional | Indented tree |
| Show geographic distribution (sensor coverage) | **Choropleth / heatmap** | Spatial intuition | Point cloud |
| Show CPS 3D structure + live data | **Digital twin 3D overlay** | Spatial alignment with physical system | AR HUD |
| Show multivariate outlier detection results | **t-SNE / UMAP projection** | Cluster emergence | Parallel coordinates |
| Show process flow / production line | **Sankey diagram** | Flow quantity encoding | Chord diagram |
| Show uncertainty in CPS predictions | **Violin plot / gradient fill** | Communicates distribution | Box plot |

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Data-Ink Ratio Optimization

**Problem Statement:** A CPS dashboard contains 1,200 ink-pixels of gridlines, borders, and decoration, and 1,800 ink-pixels of actual data marks. Compute the data-ink ratio and recommend a redesign.

**Step 1 — Recall Tufte's Data-Ink Ratio.**

$$R_{ink} = \frac{\text{Data Ink}}{\text{Total Ink}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$R_{ink} = \frac{1800}{1800 + 1200} = \frac{1800}{3000} = 0.60$$

**Step 3 — Interpret.** Tufte's ideal is $R_{ink} > 0.75$. Our 0.60 indicates **chartjunk** (decoration reducing clarity).

**Step 4 — Redesign actions.**
- Remove redundant gridlines.
- Drop the 3D effects.
- Simplify legend.
- Lighten non-data ink to background gray.

**Step 5 — Expected outcome.** $R_{ink}^{new} \geq 0.80$ improves readability and reduces cognitive load — critical for time-pressured CPS operators.
