# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Define Information Visualization in the context of Cyber-Physical Systems. List any three visualization techniques used in CPS interfaces.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Information Visualization in CPS is the structured graphical encoding of sensor data, system states, and predictive analytics into visual artifacts (charts, maps, 3D models) that enable human operators to perceive, comprehend, and act upon cyber-physical processes in real time.

**Three Techniques:**
1. **Time-series line charts** — for continuous variable monitoring (temperature, pressure, vibration).
2. **Geospatial heatmaps** — for spatial CPS such as smart grid load or traffic density.
3. **Digital twin 3D overlays** — for spatial alignment of live data with physical asset geometry.

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State Shneiderman's Mantra for visualization design. Explain how it applies to a CPS fault-monitoring dashboard.**

**Model Answer:**

**Mantra:** *"Overview first, zoom and filter, then details on demand."*

**Application to CPS Fault Dashboard:**
1. **Overview:** The dashboard opens with a system-wide health summary showing all subsystems colored by status (green/yellow/red). This is the pre-attentive global view.
2. **Zoom and Filter:** The operator clicks on a yellow "warning" subsystem to isolate it and hide unrelated data, reducing visual clutter.
3. **Details on Demand:** On hovering/clicking a specific sensor, raw waveform, alarm history, and probable root cause appear in a side panel, enabling root-cause analysis without leaving the overview context.

This mantra respects Miller's working memory limit and supports both rapid situational awareness and deep drill-down diagnostics.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **seven-stage visualization pipeline** for CPS interfaces, with an example drawn from an autonomous vehicle.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss **parallel coordinates** and **treemaps** as visualization techniques. For each, give one CPS scenario where it is the most appropriate choice.

---

#### Solution to (a) — Seven-Stage Visualization Pipeline

The visualization pipeline for a CPS interface is a structured sequence of transformations converting raw sensor data into actionable visual artifacts. Using an **autonomous vehicle** (AV) as the running example:

**Stage 1 — Data Acquisition:** Multiple sensors (LiDAR at 10 Hz, cameras at 30 Hz, radar at 20 Hz, IMU at 100 Hz, GPS at 1 Hz) stream data into the central compute unit.

**Stage 2 — Preprocessing:** Noise filtering (Kalman filter for GPS+IMU fusion), object detection on camera frames (YOLO inference), point cloud segmentation on LiDAR, ego-motion compensation.

**Stage 3 — Transformation & Aggregation:** Sensor fusion produces a unified world model — typically a 3D occupancy grid updated at 50 Hz. Trajectories of detected objects are tracked with Kalman filters.

**Stage 4 — Visual Mapping:** Object positions → x,y screen coordinates; object class → hue (cars blue, pedestrians red, cyclists yellow); speed → size; prediction confidence → opacity.

**Stage 5 — Rendering & Layout:** The 3D bird's-eye view is rendered with perspective projection. Heat overlays show planned trajectory (cyan) and predicted path of pedestrians (orange).

**Stage 6 — Interaction:** Driver can rotate the view, tap a pedestrian icon to see its predicted trajectory 3 seconds ahead, and zoom into the rear camera feed.

**Stage 7 — Display & Feedback Loop:** The visualization is shown on the central console. Driver observes an unrecognized pedestrian near a blind spot, applies brakes, vehicle responds, visualization updates in the next frame.

> This pipeline must execute within the **100 ms latency budget** typical of Level 3 autonomous driving.

---

#### Solution to (b) — Parallel Coordinates and Treemaps

**Parallel Coordinates:**
- **Definition:** A visualization where N parallel vertical axes represent N variables, and each data record is drawn as a polyline crossing each axis at its value. Patterns of crossings reveal correlations.
- **CPS Scenario:** Monitoring a 12-sensor industrial robot's joint state (positions, velocities, torques, temperatures across 6 joints plus gripper). Engineers can instantly spot anomalous joint combinations during operation.

**Treemaps:**
- **Definition:** A hierarchical visualization where space is recursively partitioned into rectangles, with each rectangle's area proportional to a quantitative attribute of that branch.
- **CPS Scenario:** Visualizing memory and CPU consumption across an embedded CPS controller's process tree. The total area equals the entire resource budget; each rectangle represents a process or task; color encodes priority or anomaly level.

**Comparative Justification:**
- Parallel coordinates excel at **multivariate comparison** where order matters and N is moderate (4–20 dimensions).
- Treemaps excel at **part-to-whole hierarchical visualization** with thousands of leaves where relative proportion matters.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With suitable diagrams, compare **2D dashboards, 3D digital twins, and AR overlays** as visualization modes for CPS. State one engineering advantage and one limitation of each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Apply **Stevens' Power Law** and **Fitts's Law** to justify design decisions for a CPS emergency-stop button on an industrial robot control panel.

---

#### Solution to (a) — Comparison of Visualization Modes

| Mode | Description | Advantage | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **2D Dashboard** | Flat panel of charts, KPIs, gauges | Familiar; high information density; low hardware cost | Lacks spatial correspondence with physical CPS |
| **3D Digital Twin** | Real-time 3D replica of physical system | Spatial alignment with physical geometry; intuitive | Requires 3D rendering budget; occlusion issues |
| **AR Overlay** | Annotations superimposed on physical view via headset | Direct contextual information; hands-free | Hardware cost; calibration drift; field-of-view limits |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Many students write *only advantages* and forget to state **limitations**. Marks are split equally between (i) clear description, (ii) one engineering advantage, and (iii) one limitation. Missing any column costs 2–3 marks per mode.

---

#### Solution to (b) — Stevens' Power Law and Fitts's Law Application

**Stevens' Power Law** states:

$$\psi = k \cdot S^{n}$$

where $\psi$ is perceived intensity, $S$ is stimulus magnitude, $k$ is a constant, and $n$ depends on the visual variable:
- Length: $n = 1.0$ (most accurate)
- Area: $n = 0.7$
- Brightness: $n = 0.33$ (least accurate)

**Design Implication for E-Stop Button:**
- Use **size scaling** rather than brightness scaling to communicate urgency — perceived size change matches physical size change (highest $n$).
- Avoid relying solely on color brightness; combine with shape (a hexagonal button, not just red color) so color-blind operators still perceive it pre-attentively.

**Fitts's Law** states:

$$T = a + b \cdot \log_2\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)$$

**Design Implication for E-Stop Button:**
- Maximize $W$ (button size) and minimize $D$ (distance from operator's resting hand position).
- Place the E-stop on the grip itself or within 10 cm reach — not on a distant panel.
- Make it raised/tactile so it is acquired without looking (eyes can stay on the dangerous operation).

**Numerical Example (Reusing Derivation 2):**
For $D = 10 \text{ cm}$, $W = 6 \text{ cm}$:

$$ID = \log_2\left(\frac{10}{6} + 1\right) = \log_2(2.667) \approx 1.415 \text{ bits}$$

$$T = 50 + 150 \cdot 1.415 = 50 + 212.3 = 262.3 \text{ ms}$$

This is **half the time** of the earlier 30 cm / 4 cm design — a life-critical improvement in CPS safety.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Common Pitfall — Visualization Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "graph" with "chart"** — graphs are network/relational; charts are statistical. Using the wrong term loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting perceptual laws** — naming a chart without justifying *why* it suits human cognition loses 3 marks.
> 3. **Ignoring real-time constraints** — CPS visualization must specify latency, refresh rate, and frame budget. Omission costs 2 marks.
> 4. **No quantitative analysis** — always include a numerical calculation (Fitts's Law, Nyquist, or Tufte ratio) to score full 14 marks in Part B.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- **Definition Anchor:** Information Visualization in CPS = graphical encoding of CPS data to amplify human cognition.
- **Pipeline:** Acquisition → Preprocessing → Transformation → Visual Mapping → Rendering → Interaction → Display + Feedback.
- **Mantra:** Shneiderman's *"Overview first, zoom and filter, then details on demand."*
- **Visual Variables:** Position, Size, Shape, Value, Color, Orientation, Texture (+ Motion).
- **Time-Series:** Line, Sparkline, Stacked Area, Gantt.
- **Geospatial:** Choropleth, Heatmap, Isarithmic.
- **Network:** Node-link, Adjacency Matrix, Arc.
- **Multivariate:** Parallel Coordinates, SPLOM, t-SNE/UMAP.
- **Hierarchical:** Treemap, Sunburst, Indented Tree.
- **Immersive:** Digital Twin 3D, AR HUD, VR Control Room.
- **Nyquist:** $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$.
- **Pre-attentive limit:** 200 ms.
- **Working memory:** 7 ± 2 chunks.
- **Stevens' Power Law:** $\psi = k S^n$; length most accurate ($n=1$), brightness least ($n=0.33$).
- **Fitts's Law:** $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(D/W + 1)$; maximize $W$, minimize $D$.
- **Tufte's Data-Ink Ratio:** $R_{ink} = \text{Data Ink} / \text{Total Ink}$; aim $> 0.75$.
- **CIEDE2000:** $\Delta E_{00} \geq 5$ for clear perceptual color separation.
- **Common Pitfalls:** Confusing graph/chart, missing perceptual laws, ignoring real-time, no numbers.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Information Visualization Techniques for CPS Interfaces**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Designing Effective Visual and Auditory Outputs for Real-Time Data in CPS
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT524 - INTERFACE DESIGN FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Designing Effective Visual and Auditory Outputs for Real-Time Data in CPS

# 🎨 Designing Effective Visual and Auditory Outputs for Real-Time Data in CPS

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Visual and Auditory Outputs in CPS** refer to the engineered sensory feedback channels through which a Cyber-Physical System communicates its operational state, processed sensor data, alerts, and decision outputs back to the human operator. In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme, these outputs are designed using principles derived from **Human-Computer Interaction (HCI)**, **Cognitive Engineering**, and **Data Visualization theory** to ensure **situation awareness**, **low cognitive load**, and **minimal reaction latency**.

A formal definition:

> **Visual Output:** Any graphical, textual, symbolic, or pictographic representation rendered on a display medium (LCD, LED, HUD, AR overlay) that conveys real-time CPS state variables.

> **Auditory Output:** Any structured acoustic signal — including alerts, alarms, speech prompts, and **sonification** mappings — emitted via speakers or transducers to convey CPS state information through the hearing channel.

The combined paradigm is called **Multi-Modal Feedback** or **Redundant Sensory Coding**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a modern car. The **speedometer** (visual gauge), the **navigation arrows** (visual HUD), the **seatbelt warning chime** (auditory beep), and the **engine revving sound** (auditory feedback) are all *outputs* designed by engineers. None of them are random — they are carefully crafted so that you can:

- **Glance and understand** the speed (visual)
- **Hear and react** to a collision warning (auditory)
- **Feel and intuit** an unsafe lane change (haptic — out of scope here)

In a CPS, the designer must make these outputs **real-time** (latency < 100 ms ideally), **legible** (correct under stress), and **non-intrusive** (no information overload).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The output modality must match the **operator's task**, **ambient environment**, and **criticality** of the CPS function. Auditory outputs dominate when the operator's *eyes are busy* (e.g., driving); visual outputs dominate when *ears are saturated* (e.g., noisy factory floor).

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

- **Latency (L):** Time between sensor event and output rendering. **Target: ≤ 100 ms** for safety-critical CPS, ≤ 250 ms for monitoring.
- **Refresh Rate (f):** Visual frame update frequency. **Standard: 30–60 Hz** for dashboards, ≥ 75 Hz for motion-critical displays.
- **Sound Pressure Level (SPL):** Loudness measured in **dB SPL**. Reference: **0 dB SPL = 20 µPa**.
- **Just-Noticeable Difference (JND):** Minimum change in stimulus the human can detect. For loudness ≈ **1 dB**, for brightness ≈ **1%**.
- **Field of View (FOV):** Angular extent of visual display. Human binocular FOV ≈ **200° horizontal**, **135° vertical**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Imperative:** A real-time CPS output must satisfy **Shannon's Human-In-The-Loop Bandwidth Equation** — the rate of information delivered (in bits/sec) must not exceed the human operator's perceptual channel capacity.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Visual Output Design

Visual outputs in CPS can be decomposed into the following hierarchy:

- **1. Static Displays:** Numerical readouts, textual labels, status icons. Low update rate, high precision.
- **2. Dynamic Graphs:** Time-series line charts, bar graphs, Gantt charts, sparklines. Ideal for **trend monitoring** of CPS variables (temperature, pressure, flow rate).
- **3. Spatial Visualizations:** 2D/3D maps, heatmaps, scatter plots, process schematics. Used when CPS data is **geo-spatial** or **topologically related**.
- **4. Gauge-Style Indicators:** Dials, tachometers, level meters, LED bar graphs. Mirrors physical instrument metaphors.
- **5. Augmented Reality (AR) Overlays:** Real-world camera view + virtual CPS annotations. Used in **Industry 4.0** and surgical CPS.
- **6. Head-Up Displays (HUD):** Projected on windshields/visors, allows hands-free operation.

### 2.2 Principles of Visual Design (Tufte + Few + KTU)

Stephen Few and Edward Tufte's principles, adapted to CPS:

1. **Data-Ink Ratio Maximization:** Remove all non-essential ink (chartjunk).
2. **Pre-attentive Attribute Usage:** Use color, size, orientation, motion — processed by the brain in < 200 ms without conscious effort.
3. **Gestalt Principles:** Proximity, similarity, closure, continuity — guide grouping.
4. **Color Theory:** Use **sequential** colormaps for ordered data, **diverging** for data with midpoint, **categorical** for nominal.
5. **Small Multiples:** Repeat visual encoding for multivariate comparisons.

### 2.3 Taxonomy of Auditory Output Design

| Type | Description | CPS Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Alarms / Beeps** | Discrete tonal signals | Threshold violation alerts |
| **Earcons** | Abstract musical motifs assigned meaning | System mode changes |
| **Auditory Icons** | Real-world sounds mapped to events | "Click" for valve closure |
| **Speech Output (TTS)** | Synthesized voice prompts | Diagnostic messages |
| **Sonification** | Continuous data → continuous sound parameter | Vibration, heart-rate, pressure trends |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sonification** is the mapping of data to non-speech audio. Parameters include **pitch (frequency)**, **loudness**, **timbre**, **rhythm**, and **spatial position (3D audio)**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Latency** | $L = t_{render} - t_{event}$ | **milliseconds (ms)** |
| **Frame Rate** | $f = \dfrac{1}{T_{frame}}$ | **Hertz (Hz)** |
| **Bandwidth Requirement** | $BW = f \times W \times H \times bpp$ | **bytes/sec** |
| **Human Visual Channel Capacity** | $C_{visual} \approx 10^7$ bits/sec (raw), $C_{perceived} \approx 50$ bits/sec | **bits/sec** |
| **Auditory SPL (dB)** | $SPL = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{p}{p_0}\right)$ | **dB SPL**, $p_0 = 20\,\mu Pa$ |
| **Pitch Frequency Range** | $f_{human} \in [20,\; 20000]$ | **Hz** |
| **JND for Loudness** | $\Delta L \approx 1$ | **dB** |
| **Fitts's Law (Pointing)** | $MT = a + b \log_2\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right)$ | **seconds**, $D$ = distance, $W$ = width |
| **Hick's Law (Choice RT)** | $RT = a + b \log_2(n+1)$ | **seconds**, $n$ = choices |
| **Sound Intensity** | $I = \dfrac{p^2}{\rho c}$ | **W/m²** |
| **Speaker SPL at distance d** | $SPL_d = SPL_0 - 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{d}{d_0}\right)$ | **dB** |
| **Information Rate (Channel)** | $R = B \log_2\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | **bits/sec** (Shannon) |
| **Critical Flicker Frequency** | $CFF \approx 60$ Hz typical | **Hz** |
| **Response Time to Visual Stimulus** | $RT_{visual} \approx 250$ ms | **ms** |
| **Response Time to Auditory Stimulus** | $RT_{auditory} \approx 170$ ms | **ms** |
| **Stevens' Power Law (Loudness)** | $\psi = k (I - I_0)^n$ | $n \approx 0.67$ for loudness |
| **Stevens' Power Law (Brightness)** | $\psi = k (I - I_0)^n$ | $n \approx 0.33$ for brightness |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Auditory stimuli have a **faster reaction time (~170 ms)** than visual stimuli (~250 ms). This justifies auditory alarms in safety-critical CPS where milliseconds matter (e.g., collision avoidance, nuclear shutdown).

### 2.5 Why Multi-Modal Output Design Matters in CPS

CPS is by definition **closed-loop** — the human is in the loop. Output design directly determines:

- **Situation Awareness (SA):** Per Endsley's 3-level model (perception → comprehension → projection).
- **Cognitive Load Reduction:** Offload working memory to perception.
- **Error Prevention:** Right output at right time prevents mode-confusion (e.g., Therac-25 tragedy).
- **Trust Calibration:** Well-designed outputs build calibrated trust — not too much, not too little.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Shannon-Hartley Theorem Applied to Human Output Channel

The Shannon-Hartley theorem states the maximum data rate over a noisy channel:

$$C = B \log_2\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$$

Where:
- $C$ = channel capacity (bits/sec)
- $B$ = channel bandwidth (Hz)
- $S/N$ = signal-to-noise ratio (dimensionless)

**Step-by-step Application to Visual Output Channel of a CPS Operator:**

**Step 1:** Identify the channel bandwidth.
The human visual system's effective temporal bandwidth is approximately:

$$B_{visual} \approx 40 \text{ Hz}$$

This is because the human eye's *critical flicker frequency* is ~60 Hz, and perception plateaus at ~40 Hz usable.

**Step 2:** Identify the signal-to-noise ratio under typical CPS conditions.

For a well-lit control room, $S/N \approx 1000$ (30 dB). For a high-noise factory, $S/N \approx 10$ (10 dB).

**Step 3:** Compute channel capacity under controlled room conditions:

$$C_{controlled} = 40 \times \log_2(1 + 1000)$$

Evaluate:

$$\log_2(1001) \approx 9.97$$

$$C_{controlled} \approx 40 \times 9.97 \approx 398.8 \text{ bits/sec}$$

**Step 4:** Compute channel capacity under noisy factory:

$$C_{noisy} = 40 \times \log_2(1 + 10)$$

Evaluate:

$$\log_2(11) \approx 3.46$$

$$C_{noisy} \approx 40 \times 3.46 \approx 138.4 \text{ bits/sec}$$

**Step 5:** Conclusion.

The visual channel capacity **drops by ~65%** when ambient noise degrades the signal. This quantitatively justifies adding **redundant auditory output** in noisy CPS environments.

### 3.2 Derivation: Optimal Sonification Mapping for Pressure Data

Suppose a CPS pressure sensor outputs values in the range $[0, 100]$ bar. We wish to map this to audible frequency $f \in [200, 2000]$ Hz.

**Step 1:** Define linear mapping.

$$f(p) = f_{min} + \dfrac{(f_{max} - f_{min})}{p_{max} - p_{min}} (p - p_{min})$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.

$$f(p) = 200 + \dfrac{2000 - 200}{100 - 0} (p - 0)$$

Simplify:

$$f(p) = 200 + 18 \cdot p$$

**Step 3:** Verify boundary conditions.

For $p = 0$: $f = 200$ Hz ✓
For $p = 100$: $f = 200 + 1800 = 2000$ Hz ✓

**Step 4:** Compute sensitivity (derivative).

$$\dfrac{df}{dp} = 18 \text{ Hz/bar}$$

This means every 1-bar change produces an 18-Hz shift, well above the JND for pitch discrimination (~3 Hz at 1 kHz), ensuring **detectability**.

**Step 5:** Engineering judgment.

If 18 Hz/bar is too coarse for fine-grained monitoring, we can use a **logarithmic mapping** (Stevens' Power Law) to compress low values:

$$f(p) = f_{min} \cdot \left(\dfrac{f_{max}}{f_{min}}\right)^{\frac{p}{p_{max}}}$$

Plug in:

$$f(p) = 200 \cdot 10^{\frac{p}{100} \cdot 1}$$

$$f(50) = 200 \cdot 10^{0.5} \approx 200 \cdot 3.162 \approx 632.5 \text{ Hz}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Modality Selection Heuristics

| CPS Scenario | Primary Modality | Secondary Modality | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Autonomous vehicle (driver must react) | **Auditory alarm** | Visual icon | Faster RT (170 ms vs 250 ms) |
| Power plant control room | **Visual trend graph** | Subtle auditory tick | Eyes free; ears occupied by alarms |
| Surgical robot (surgeon eyes on patient) | **Auditory + speech** | Tactile (out of scope) | Eyes cannot leave operative field |
| Smart home thermostat | **Visual e-ink display** | None | Low-criticality, ambient awareness |
| UAV swarm control | **Visual 3D map + AR** | Spatial 3D audio | Spatial cues disambiguate drones |
| Industrial IoT predictive maintenance | **Visual dashboard + vibration sonification** | Email/TTS | Long-duration trend + anomaly detection |
| Patient ICU monitoring | **Visual waveform + auditory alarms** | None | Multi-vital, life-critical |
| Railway signal control | **Visual light signal** | Audible horn | Legacy + redundancy |

### 3.4 Derivation: Frame Bandwidth for a CPS Dashboard

Consider a 1920×1080 dashboard, 24-bit color, 30 Hz refresh.

**Step 1:** Compute pixels per frame.

$$N_{pix} = 1920 \times 1080 = 2{,}073{,}600 \text{ pixels}$$

**Step 2:** Compute bits per frame.

$$B_{frame} = N_{pix} \times bpp = 2{,}073{,}600 \times 24 = 49{,}766{,}400 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3:** Compute bandwidth.

$$BW = f \times B_{frame} = 30 \times 49{,}766{,}400 = 1.493 \times 10^9 \text{ bits/sec}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to practical units.

$$BW \approx 1.49 \text{ Gbps}$$

This bandwidth exceeds the human operator's perceptual capacity ($C_{perceived} \approx 50$ bits/sec) by a factor of **~30 million**. This implies designers must **aggressively filter** and **aggregate** before display.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Rule:** Information rate presented to the human should be **≤ 50 bits/sec** to avoid cognitive overload, regardless of how much raw data the CPS processes internally.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: CPS Output Design Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["CPS DATA SOURCES"]
        sensorA[Sensor Stream A]
        sensorB[Sensor Stream B]
        sensorC[Sensor Stream C]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS["PROCESSING LAYER"]
        filter[Data Filter]
        aggregate[Aggregator]
        feature[Feature Extractor]
        priority[Priority Classifier]
    end

    subgraph DECISION["MODALITY SELECTOR"]
        visualDec{Visual Critical?}
        audioDec{Audio Critical?}
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT CHANNELS"]
        visEngine[Visual Renderer]
        audEngine[Auditory Renderer]
        speaker[Speakers]
        display[Display Screen]
    end

    subgraph HUMAN["HUMAN OPERATOR"]
        eyes[Visual Cortex]
        ears[Auditory Cortex]
        brain[Decision Center]
        response[Action Output]
    end

    sensorA --> filter
    sensorB --> filter
    sensorC --> filter
    filter --> aggregate
    aggregate --> feature
    feature --> priority
    priority --> visualDec
    priority --> audioDec
    visualDec -- Yes --> visEngine
    audioDec -- Yes --> audEngine
    visEngine --> display
    audEngine --> speaker
    display --> eyes
    speaker --> ears
    eyes --> brain
    ears --> brain
    brain --> response
    response --> CPS[CPS Actuator Feedback]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Visual Output Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    visRoot[Visual Output Categories]
    visRoot --> static[Static Displays]
    visRoot --> dynamic[Dynamic Graphs]
    visRoot --> spatial[Spatial Maps]
    visRoot --> gauge[Gauge Indicators]
    visRoot --> ar[AR Overlays]
    visRoot --> hud[HUD Projections]

    static --> sNum[Numerical Readouts]
    static --> sTxt[Textual Labels]
    static --> sIcon[Status Icons]

    dynamic --> dLine[Line Charts]
    dynamic --> dBar[Bar Graphs]
    dynamic --> dSpark[Sparklines]
    dynamic --> dHeat[Heatmaps]

    spatial --> sp2D[2D Maps]
    spatial --> sp3D[3D Topologies]
    spatial --> spScat[Scatter Plots]

    gauge --> gDial[Dials]
    gauge --> gTach[Tachometers]
    gauge --> gLED[LED Bars]

    ar --> arInd[Industrial AR]
    ar --> arMed[Surgical AR]
    ar --> arAuto[Automotive AR]

    hud --> hudCar[Automotive HUD]
    hud --> hudPilot[Aviator HUD]
    hud --> hudHelm[Helmet-Mounted]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Auditory Output Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    audStart[Auditory Output Needed?]
    audStart -- Yes --> q1{Operator Eyes Busy?}
    q1 -- Yes --> q2{Discrete or Continuous?}
    q2 -- Discrete --> alarm[Use Alarms / Beeps]
    q2 -- Continuous --> son[Use Sonification]
    q1 -- No --> q3{Multiple Identical Events?}
    q3 -- Yes --> ear[Use Earcons]
    q3 -- No --> q4{Real-World Mapping Possible?}
    q4 -- Yes --> aIcon[Use Auditory Icons]
    q4 -- No --> q5{Verbal Message Needed?}
    q5 -- Yes --> tts[Use Speech TTS]
    q5 -- No --> ear
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Multi-Modal Feedback Loop in CPS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    physWorld[Physical World]
    sensorNode[Sensor]
    ctrlUnit[Controller]
    visOut[Visual Output]
    audOut[Auditory Output]
    operator[Human Operator]
    actuator[Actuator]

    physWorld --> sensorNode
    sensorNode --> ctrlUnit
    ctrlUnit --> visOut
    ctrlUnit --> audOut
    visOut --> operator
    audOut --> operator
    operator --> actuator
    actuator --> physWorld
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PECPT524]**

Define **sonification** in the context of CPS interface design. Mention any two parameters of sound used for data mapping.

**Model Answer:**

Sonification is the systematic mapping of data variables to non-speech audio parameters so that an operator can monitor continuous CPS processes through the auditory channel. Two commonly used parameters are:

1. **Pitch (Frequency)** — Used to represent magnitude (e.g., higher pressure = higher frequency).
2. **Loudness (Amplitude / SPL)** — Used to represent intensity or proximity to a threshold.

*Other acceptable parameters: timbre, rhythm, spatial position (3D audio).*

---

#### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PECPT524]**

State **Hick's Law** and explain its relevance in designing visual output menus for CPS dashboards.

**Model Answer:**

Hick's Law states:

$$RT = a + b \log_2(n+1)$$

Where $RT$ is reaction time, $n$ is the number of equally probable choices, and $a$, $b$ are empirical constants.

**Relevance:** As the number of menu options in a CPS dashboard increases, the operator's reaction time grows logarithmically. Therefore, designers must limit the number of simultaneously visible choices (typically $n \leq 7 \pm 2$) to keep response time low and prevent decision paralysis in time-critical CPS operations.

---

### 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PECPT524, Module 3]**

**(a)** With the aid of a block diagram, explain the **multi-modal feedback architecture** for a Cyber-Physical System. Discuss how visual and auditory outputs complement each other in enhancing situation awareness. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A CPS pressure sensor outputs values in the range $[0, 50]$ bar. Design a **linear sonification mapping** to audible frequency in the range $[300, 1500]$ Hz. Calculate the frequency when pressure is 35 bar. Verify whether this mapping respects the human **JND for pitch discrimination** (~3 Hz). **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Multi-Modal Feedback Architecture:**

The architecture consists of three functional layers:

1. **Sensor / Data Layer:** Multiple CPS sensors (temperature, pressure, flow, position) stream data to a central controller.
2. **Processing Layer:** The controller filters noise, aggregates trends, extracts features, and prioritizes critical events.
3. **Output Layer:** Two parallel renderers — a **Visual Renderer** (driving LCDs, LEDs, HUDs) and an **Auditory Renderer** (driving speakers, earphones, buzzers).

```
Sensors → Processor → Visual Renderer → Display → Operator (Eyes)
                  ↘  Auditory Renderer → Speaker → Operator (Ears)
                                          ↘ Operator Decision → Actuator
```

**Complementarity:**

- **Redundancy:** Same event signaled via two channels increases reliability (one may fail or be obstructed).
- **Speed:** Auditory RT (~170 ms) is faster than visual RT (~250 ms), so auditory dominates for *urgency*.
- **Bandwidth Splitting:** Reduces cognitive load by distributing information across sensory modalities.
- **Context-Awareness:** In dim or noisy environments, auditory survives; in loud environments, visual survives.

**(b) Linear Sonification Mapping:**

**Step 1:** Write the linear mapping formula.

$$f(p) = f_{min} + \dfrac{f_{max} - f_{min}}{p_{max} - p_{min}} (p - p_{min})$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values: $f_{min}=300$, $f_{max}=1500$, $p_{min}=0$, $p_{max}=50$.

$$f(p) = 300 + \dfrac{1500 - 300}{50 - 0} (p - 0)$$

Simplify:

$$f(p) = 300 + 24 \cdot p$$

**Step 3:** Compute $f$ at $p = 35$ bar.

$$f(35) = 300 + 24 \times 35 = 300 + 840 = 1140 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the sensitivity (rate of change).

$$\dfrac{df}{dp} = 24 \text{ Hz/bar}$$

**Step 5:** Compare with JND.

Since the JND for pitch discrimination near 1 kHz is approximately 3 Hz, and our mapping produces a **24 Hz change per 1-bar change**, the operator will easily detect even small pressure variations of **0.125 bar**. Hence the mapping **respects the JND constraint** by a factor of 8×.

**Conclusion:** The linear mapping is perceptually safe and operationally effective.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PECPT524, Module 3]**

**(a)** List and explain **any four principles** of effective visual output design for real-time CPS dashboards. For each principle, give a CPS example. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare **visual vs auditory outputs** in CPS using a suitable analytical matrix. Justify the choice of modality for the following CPS scenarios: **(i)** autonomous vehicle collision warning, **(ii)** ICU heart-rate monitor, **(iii)** nuclear plant pressure control. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Four Principles of Visual Output Design:**

1. **Pre-attentive Attribute Usage:** Use attributes like color, size, and motion that the brain processes in <200 ms subconsciously.
   *CPS Example:* A red flashing icon indicates a critical alarm in a SCADA system without the operator needing to read text.

2. **Data-Ink Ratio Maximization (Tufte):** Remove all decorative elements that do not convey information.
   *CPS Example:* Strip gridlines, backgrounds, 3D effects from a power-grid load graph to focus only on the trend line.

3. **Gestalt Principle of Similarity:** Group related CPS variables using shared color or shape.
   *CPS Example:* All temperature sensors in a chemical plant are rendered in shades of red; pressure sensors in blue.

4. **Small Multiples (Tufte):** Repeat the same visual encoding for multivariate comparison.
   *CPS Example:* Show 12 sparklines of bearing temperature in a wind turbine side by side for instant comparison.

*Other acceptable principles: hierarchical layering, color-blind safe palettes, bounded scales, consistent units.*

**(b) Comparison Matrix:**

| Property | Visual Output | Auditory Output |
|---|---|---|
| Reaction Time | ~250 ms | ~170 ms |
| Information Density | High | Low |
| Operator Eyes Required? | Yes | No |
| Operator Ears Required? | No | Yes |
| Suitable for Continuous Data? | Excellent | Excellent (sonification) |
| Suitable for Alarms? | Good | Excellent |
| Affected by Noise? | Less | Highly |
| Affected by Lighting? | Highly | Less |
| Spatial Resolution | Excellent | Possible (3D audio) |
| Memory Demand | Low (persistent) | Transient |

**Scenario Justifications:**

**(i) Autonomous Vehicle Collision Warning → Auditory Primary + Visual Secondary.**
Auditory beep has faster RT (~170 ms), critical in collision avoidance. Visual icon on dashboard supplements for confirmation. Auditory is mandatory because the driver's eyes are on the road.

**(ii) ICU Heart-Rate Monitor → Visual Primary (Waveform) + Auditory Alarms (Threshold breach).**
Continuous waveform on the monitor allows trend interpretation. Audible alarm only triggers when heart rate crosses thresholds (e.g., >120 bpm), preserving silence during normal operation.

**(iii) Nuclear Plant Pressure Control → Visual Primary (Trends + Gauges) + Auditory Backup.**
Operators must read precise numerical values and trend curves visually. Auditory alarms serve as redundant backup in case the visual display fails. Criticality demands both, with visual dominant because quantitative accuracy is paramount.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> - Students often confuse **sonification** with **auditory icons**. Sonification maps *continuous data* to *continuous audio parameters*; auditory icons map *discrete events* to *real-world recorded sounds*. Do not interchange.
> - Hick's Law applies to **choice reaction time**, not simple reaction time. Many students wrongly cite it for alarm response.
> - Fitts's Law is for **pointing/motor movement**, not visual perception. Use it for *target selection* in CPS interfaces.
> - Auditory reaction time is **170 ms**, not 100 ms. Do not overstate.
> - In multi-modal design, mention **redundancy gain** and **complementarity**, not just "both used together".
> - Always specify **JND values numerically** when justifying perceptual mappings.

---

### ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Real-time CPS outputs** must satisfy latency ≤ 100 ms for safety-critical, ≤ 250 ms otherwise.
- **Visual outputs** are categorized into static, dynamic, spatial, gauge, AR, and HUD.
- **Auditory outputs** are categorized into alarms, earcons, auditory icons, TTS, and sonification.
- **Sonification** maps continuous data → continuous sound parameters (pitch, loudness, timbre, rhythm).
- **Hick's Law** $RT = a + b\log_2(n+1)$ governs choice reaction time → limit menu options.
- **Fitts's Law** $MT = a + b\log_2(D/W + 1)$ governs pointing time → make targets large.
- **Shannon-Hartley Theorem** $C = B\log_2(1+S/N)$ quantifies channel capacity.
- **Auditory RT (170 ms) < Visual RT (250 ms)** — auditory preferred for urgency.
- **JND for pitch ≈ 3 Hz at 1 kHz; for loudness ≈ 1 dB.**
- **Multi-modal feedback** provides redundancy, speed, and bandwidth splitting.
- **Stevens' Power Law** governs perceptual scaling: $n = 0.67$ for loudness, $n = 0.33$ for brightness.
- **Always design to ≤ 50 bits/sec perceptual channel capacity** to avoid cognitive overload.
- **Endsley's 3-level Situation Awareness Model** (perception → comprehension → projection) guides output design.
- **Tufte's principles:** Data-ink ratio, small multiples, pre-attentive attributes.
- **Color theory:** Sequential (ordered), Diverging (midpoint), Categorical (nominal) colormaps.
- **3D spatial audio** is increasingly used in UAV swarm CPS for drone disambiguation.
- **Therac-25 lesson:** Poor interface outputs led to radiation overdose — design outputs to *prevent*, not *enable*, errors.
- **Frame bandwidth formula:** $BW = f \times W \times H \times bpp$ — exceeds human capacity by orders of magnitude, so **filtering is mandatory**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Usability and Accessibility in CPS Interfaces: Usability Principles Specific to CPS Environments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT524 - INTERFACE DESIGN FOR CPS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Usability and Accessibility in CPS Interfaces: Usability Principles Specific to CPS Environments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Usability in Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)** refers to the degree to which a human operator can effectively, efficiently, and satisfactorily interact with a system that tightly couples computational algorithms, network communication, and physical processes to achieve specified operational goals under real-time, safety-critical, and context-sensitive conditions.

According to the **ISO 9241-11:2018** standard, usability is characterized by three measurable dimensions:

$$ Usability = f(\text{Effectiveness}, \text{Efficiency}, \text{Satisfaction}) $$

However, in CPS environments, this classical definition is extended to include a **fourth dimension — Safety**, because physical harm to humans, equipment, or the environment can result from interface mis-design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Usability principles in CPS must account for **real-time constraints, mixed-initiative control, multi-modal interaction, fault tolerance, and safety-critical feedback loops** — features absent in traditional HCI.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine driving a **modern aircraft cockpit**. The pilot is simultaneously monitoring 50+ dials, communicating with ATC, controlling flight surfaces, and managing fuel systems. If the interface shows information inconsistently or with a 2-second delay (which is fine on a website but catastrophic in flight), the consequence is not a "frustrated user" — it is a **crash**.

**CPS usability = Traditional Usability + Life-Critical Real-Time Responsiveness**

In contrast, designing the user interface for a **smartphone weather app** is traditional HCI. If the app lags, the user is annoyed. In CPS, the user (operator) may be a nuclear plant technician, an autonomous vehicle supervisor, or a surgeon controlling a robotic arm — and lag, ambiguity, or information overload can cause **physical damage or death**.

### 1.3 Why CPS Usability is Fundamentally Different

| Aspect | Traditional HCI | CPS Interface |
|---|---|---|
| Time Sensitivity | Best-effort | Hard real-time deadlines |
| Consequence of Error | User frustration | Physical harm / system damage |
| Input Modalities | Keyboard / mouse | Touch, gesture, voice, haptic, EEG |
| Operator Attention | Sustained | Split across physical & cyber tasks |
| Feedback Loop | Seconds | Milliseconds |
| Safety Standard | ISO 9241 | ISO 9241 + IEC 61508 (Functional Safety) |

> [!NOTE]
> A core metric in CPS usability is the **Response Time Budget (RTB)** — the maximum allowed delay between user action and system feedback, typically governed by the **Murphy's Law of CPS**: *"If the user can misinterpret the state, they will — at the worst possible moment."*

### 1.4 Core Principles Overview (Adapted from Nielsen & Shneiderman)

The classical usability heuristics must be **augmented** with CPS-specific principles:

1. **Visibility of System State (CPS-augmented):** Real-time display of physical state, not just cyber state.
2. **Match Between System and Real World:** Use domain-specific mental models (e.g., mimic the cockpit layout pilots are trained on).
3. **User Control and Freedom:** Emergency override must be **always reachable in ≤ 1 second** (the "Panic Button Principle").
4. **Consistency and Standards:** Cross-platform consistency across all CPS nodes.
5. **Error Prevention:** Predict and prevent unsafe actions **before** execution.
6. **Recognition Rather than Recall:** Critical information must be visible, not memorized.
7. **Flexibility and Efficiency:** Support both novice (guided) and expert (shortcut) modes.
8. **Aesthetic and Minimalist Design:** Reduce cognitive load during high-stress operations.
9. **Help Users Recognize, Diagnose, and Recover from Errors:** Graceful degradation.
10. **Help and Documentation:** Context-sensitive, voice-activated help.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield:** The examiner frequently tests the **10 Nielsen Heuristics** + their **CPS-specific extensions** (especially visibility of physical state, panic button principle, and graceful degradation).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The CPS Usability Engineering Framework

A rigorous CPS usability framework has **four layers**, each requiring distinct design principles:

#### Layer 1: Cognitive Load Management
- **Principle:** Human working memory holds **7 ± 2 chunks** (Miller's Law).
- In CPS, operators are often overwhelmed; the interface must externalize cognition.
- **Mechanism:** Use progressive disclosure, dashboards, and situation-awareness displays (SAD).

#### Layer 2: Real-Time Feedback Architecture
- **Principle:** Feedback must arrive within the system's **control loop period** $T_c$.
- **CPS Timing Constraint:**
$$ T_{\text{feedback}} \leq T_c = \frac{1}{f_{\text{control}}} $$
- where $f_{\text{control}}$ is the dominant control loop frequency (e.g., 100 Hz for robotic arms → 10 ms budget).

#### Layer 3: Safety-Critical Interaction Design
- **Principle:** Every user action must be classifiable into **Safe / Caution / Unsafe**.
- Unsafe actions require **confirmation + reason** (the "Two-Step Rule").
- **Formal Safety Integrity Level (SIL):** Governed by **IEC 61508**.

#### Layer 4: Accessibility & Universal Design
- **Principle:** Interface must adapt to operator capabilities (vision, motor, cognitive).
- Governed by **WCAG 2.2** (Web Content Accessibility Guidelines) extended to CPS modalities.

### 2.2 Shneiderman's 8 Golden Rules (CPS Adaptation)

| Rule | Classical HCI Meaning | CPS-Specific Augmentation |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Consistency | Same action → same result | **Cross-modal consistency** (voice command = gesture) |
| 2. Shortcuts | Empower experts | **Emergency shortcuts** (single-key abort) |
| 3. Informative Feedback | Acknowledge every action | **Sub-100ms feedback** for safety-critical |
| 4. Dialog with Closure | Clear task progression | **Mission-phase awareness** (start, mid, end states) |
| 5. Error Handling | Prevent/recover | **Predictive error prevention** (AI-driven) |
| 6. Reversible Actions | Undo | **Rollback to safe state** (not just last action) |
| 7. Internal Locus of Control | User feels in charge | **Shared autonomy** (human + AI co-pilot) |
| 8. Reduce Short-Term Memory | Display, don't recall | **Heads-up displays (HUDs)** + AR overlays |

### 2.3 Nielsen's 10 Heuristics — CPS Mapped

1. **Visibility of System Status** → Display both cyber state (CPU, network) **and** physical state (temperature, pressure).
2. **Match System & Real World** → Use physical metaphors (e.g., a lever icon for valve control).
3. **User Control & Freedom** → "Panic button" or **emergency stop (e-stop)** always 1-tap away.
4. **Consistency & Standards** → Use **domain conventions** (e.g., red = stop, green = go in industrial CPS).
5. **Error Prevention** → Disable unsafe options **before** they can be selected.
6. **Recognition > Recall** → Show all active parameters on-screen; minimize menus.
7. **Flexibility & Efficiency** → Customizable layouts for different operator roles.
8. **Minimalist Design** → During alarms, **hide non-critical information** (focus mode).
9. **Error Recovery** → Provide **diagnostic hints**, not just error codes.
10. **Help & Documentation** → Context-sensitive, **voice-activated** (hands-free in CPS).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Formula / Definition | Unit / Constraint | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| $T_c$ | Control loop period $= 1/f_c$ | seconds | Real-time feedback budget |
| $T_{\text{RTB}}$ | Response Time Budget | $\leq T_c$ | UI must not violate |
| $C_{\text{load}}$ | Cognitive Load $= I_{\text{displayed}} / W_m$ | chunks | Miller's Law check |
| $W_m$ | Working memory capacity | $\approx 7 \pm 2$ | Miller (1956) |
| $R_{\text{mission}}$ | Mission Reliability | dimensionless | Must approach **1.0** for SIL-4 |
| $P_{\text{human error}}$ | Human Error Probability | $10^{-x}$ | IEC 61508 SIL rating |
| $T_{\text{estop}}$ | Emergency stop latency | $\leq 100$ ms | Hard constraint |
| $S$ | Satisfaction score | 1–7 Likert | SUS questionnaire |
| $E_{\text{eff}}$ | Efficiency (task time) | seconds | ISO 9241-11 metric |
| $E_{\text{ffc}}$ | Effectiveness (task success) | \% | ISO 9241-11 metric |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Murphy's Law of CPS HCI:** $T_{\text{feedback}} > T_c \Rightarrow$ System is **unsafe**, regardless of how "pretty" the UI is.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Avionics:** DO-178C + DO-254 govern UI safety in aircraft cockpits.
- **Medical CPS:** FDA's Human Factors and Usability Engineering (IEC 62366-1) governs surgical robot UIs.
- **Industrial IoT:** ISA-101.01 Human-Machine Interfaces for Process Automation Systems.
- **Automotive:** ISO 26262 + driver distraction guidelines (NHTSA 15-second rule).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Cognitive Load Compliance Check for a CPS Interface

**Problem Setup:** A CPS control room displays $I$ information items to the operator. Per Miller's Law, working memory holds $W_m$ chunks. Determine whether the interface complies with cognitive load constraints.

**Step 1 — State Miller's Law:**

$$ W_m = 7 \pm 2 \text{ chunks} $$

**Step 2 — Define Cognitive Load Metric:**

$$ C_{\text{load}} = \frac{I}{W_m} $$

where $I$ is the number of distinct information elements the operator must process simultaneously.

**Step 3 — Compliance Condition:**

$$ C_{\text{load}} \leq 1 \Rightarrow I \leq 9 \text{ (max chunks)} $$

**Step 4 — CPS Augmentation:** In CPS, $W_m$ is reduced under stress by a **stress factor** $\alpha$:

$$ W_m^{\text{CPS}} = \alpha \cdot W_m, \quad 0.5 \leq \alpha \leq 1.0 $$

Empirically, $\alpha \approx 0.6$ during high-stress CPS operations (e.g., emergency).

**Step 5 — Revised CPS Compliance:**

$$ I \leq \alpha \cdot W_m = 0.6 \times 7 = 4.2 \text{ chunks} $$

**Step 6 — Engineering Decision Rule:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I \leq 4 &\Rightarrow \text{SAFE — single-screen display sufficient} \\
4 < I \leq 9 &\Rightarrow \text{CAUTION — requires progressive disclosure} \\
I > 9 &\Rightarrow \text{UNSAFE — redesign required}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Real-Time Feedback Budget for a Robotic Surgery CPS

**Step 1 — Identify control loop frequency:**

$$ f_c = 1 \text{ kHz} \quad (\text{typical surgical robot}) $$

**Step 2 — Compute control period:**

$$ T_c = \frac{1}{f_c} = \frac{1}{1000} = 0.001 \text{ s} = 1 \text{ ms} $$

**Step 3 — Define response time budget:**

$$ T_{\text{RTB}} \leq T_c = 1 \text{ ms} $$

**Step 4 — Break down the budget across UI pipeline:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{RTB}} = T_{\text{sensor}} + T_{\text{compute}} + T_{\text{render}} + T_{\text{display}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Typical breakdown:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{sensor}} &= 0.2 \text{ ms} \\
T_{\text{compute}} &= 0.3 \text{ ms} \\
T_{\text{render}} &= 0.2 \text{ ms} \\
T_{\text{display}} &= 0.3 \text{ ms} \\
\hline
T_{\text{total}} &= 1.0 \text{ ms} = T_c \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Safety margin rule:** In safety-critical CPS, the **75% rule** applies:

$$ T_{\text{allocated}} \leq 0.75 \cdot T_c $$

leaving 25% headroom for jitter, network delay, and computational spikes.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: CPS Usability Principle Compliance Checklist

| Principle | Measurable Metric | Pass Criterion | Common Failure Mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| Real-time feedback | $T_{\text{RTB}}$ | $\leq T_c$ | UI thread starvation |
| Cognitive load | $C_{\text{load}}$ | $\leq 1$ (relaxed to 0.6 under stress) | Information overload during alarms |
| Emergency stop | $T_{\text{estop}}$ | $\leq 100$ ms | E-stop hidden in submenu |
| Error prevention | $P_{\text{human error}}$ | $\leq 10^{-3}$ (SIL-2) | Confirmations bypassed by expert mode |
| Multi-modal consistency | $\sigma_{\text{cross-modal}}$ | $\leq 50$ ms drift | Voice command lags gesture |
| Accessibility (vision) | Contrast ratio | $\geq 4.5{:}1$ (WCAG AA) | Low-contrast alarm text |
| Accessibility (motor) | Tap target size | $\geq 9 \text{ mm} \times 9 \text{ mm}$ | Tiny buttons on touch HMI |
| Satisfaction | SUS score | $\geq 68$ (industry avg) | Operators bypass UI for manual control |

> [!NOTE]
> The **System Usability Scale (SUS)** by John Brooke (1996) is the de facto standard for measuring CPS operator satisfaction. A score $\geq 80$ is considered excellent for safety-critical systems.

### 3.4 The Three-State Safety Model (CPS Specific)

Unlike classical UIs (which have **two states**: enabled / disabled), CPS interfaces use a **three-state model**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{State 1 (Safe):} &\quad \text{Action allowed, no warning} \\
\text{State 2 (Caution):} &\quad \text{Action allowed with confirmation + justification log} \\
\text{State 3 (Unsafe):} &\quad \text{Action blocked, requires supervisor override}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Mathematical Formulation:**

$$ A_{\text{state}} = f(R_{\text{risk}}, S_{\text{SIL}}, C_{\text{context}}) $$

where:
- $R_{\text{risk}}$ = risk score (0–10)
- $S_{\text{SIL}}$ = Safety Integrity Level (1–4)
- $C_{\text{context}}$ = current operational context

**State classification rule:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{risk}} \cdot S_{\text{SIL}} &\leq 3 \quad \Rightarrow \text{State 1 (Safe)} \\
3 < R_{\text{risk}} \cdot S_{\text{SIL}} &\leq 7 \quad \Rightarrow \text{State 2 (Caution)} \\
R_{\text{risk}} \cdot S_{\text{SIL}} &> 7 \quad \Rightarrow \text{State 3 (Unsafe)}
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

Explain the **three-state safety model** used in CPS interfaces. How does it differ from the traditional two-state (enabled/disabled) model?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **three-state safety model** classifies every user action into one of three categories based on the risk score $R_{\text{risk}}$ and the Safety Integrity Level $S_{\text{SIL}}$:

1. **State 1 — Safe:** Action is allowed silently without any warning. Used for routine, low-risk operations (e.g., reading a sensor value).
2. **State 2 — Caution:** Action is allowed only after the operator provides explicit confirmation and the system logs the justification. Used for moderate-risk actions (e.g., changing a setpoint).
3. **State 3 — Unsafe:** Action is **blocked** by default and requires a **supervisor override** to execute. Used for high-risk actions that could cause physical harm (e.g., opening a high-pressure valve).

**Difference from Traditional Two-State Model:**

The traditional HCI uses only **enabled/disabled** (binary). The CPS three-state model introduces an **intermediate caution state** with mandatory justification, recognizing that in safety-critical systems, "disabled" actions are sometimes necessary and "enabled" actions sometimes require verification. This reduces the risk of both *false positives* (over-restriction, operator bypasses UI) and *false negatives* (under-protection, accident occurs).

The classification rule is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{risk}} \cdot S_{\text{SIL}} &\leq 3 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Safe} \\
3 < R_{\text{risk}} \cdot S_{\text{SIL}} &\leq 7 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Caution} \\
R_{\text{risk}} \cdot S_{\text{SIL}} &> 7 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Unsafe}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

List any **five CPS-specific extensions** to Nielsen's 10 usability heuristics with a one-line justification for each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks = 5 × 0.5 + 0.5 intro):**

1. **Visibility of Physical State (not just cyber state):** The UI must show real-time physical parameters (temperature, pressure, position) — not just network/CPU status. *Why:* CPS couples cyber and physical; operators must see both.

2. **Panic Button Principle:** Emergency stop must be reachable in **≤ 1 second / ≤ 1 tap** from any screen. *Why:* In CPS, latency in abort = physical harm.

3. **Cross-Modal Consistency:** Voice, gesture, and touch commands must produce **identical** effects with **< 50 ms drift**. *Why:* Operators switch modalities under stress; inconsistency causes confusion.

4. **Predictive Error Prevention:** AI must **predict** and **block** unsafe actions **before** confirmation. *Why:* Confirmation dialogs alone are insufficient in millisecond-scale CPS.

5. **Graceful Degradation on Failure:** When sensors fail, the UI must **clearly indicate partial information** rather than hide data. *Why:* Hiding data causes operators to assume "all is well" → catastrophe.

6. **Sub-100ms Feedback for Safety-Critical Actions:** Feedback for e-stop, override, and alarm acknowledgment must be **< 100 ms**. *Why:* Human reaction time in emergencies is ~250 ms; UI must not be the bottleneck.

*(Any five well-explained points fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Discuss in detail how **Nielsen's 10 usability heuristics** are adapted for **CPS environments**, giving CPS-specific examples for at least **six heuristics**.

**(b)** [7 Marks] With the help of a **block diagram**, explain the **real-time feedback architecture** in a CPS interface. Derive the relationship between the **response time budget ($T_{\text{RTB}}$)** and the **control loop period ($T_c$)** for a typical robotic manipulator with $f_c = 500$ Hz.

---

**Model Answer for Question A:**

**(a) Nielsen's 10 Heuristics — CPS Adaptations (7 Marks):**

| # | Heuristic | Classical Meaning | CPS Adaptation with Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Visibility of system status | Show what the system is doing | **Example:** Smart grid dashboard must display both **cyber state** (server load) and **physical state** (line voltage, transformer temperature) in real time. |
| 2 | Match system & real world | Speak the user's language | **Example:** Surgical robot UI uses anatomical icons (heart, lung) rather than abstract menus. |
| 3 | User control & freedom | Provide undo/redo | **CPS Extension — Panic Button Principle:** E-stop button is always visible, always 1-tap reachable, regardless of current screen. |
| 4 | Consistency & standards | Same words/actions = same meaning | **Example:** Red always means STOP across all CPS nodes (valves, motors, alarms) — follows ANSI/ISA standards. |
| 5 | Error prevention | Prevent problems before they occur | **Example:** A nuclear plant UI **disables** the "open valve" button when pressure > safety threshold. |
| 6 | Recognition > recall | Show options, don't make user remember | **Example:** Autonomous vehicle HUD displays all active ADAS features (lane-keep, AEB, ACC) as visible icons. |
| 7 | Flexibility & efficiency | Accelerators for experts | **Example:** Expert mode allows voice command "DUMP TANK 3" while novice mode requires multi-step confirmation. |
| 8 | Minimalist design | No irrelevant info | **CPS Extension — Focus Mode:** During alarms, non-critical panels auto-hide to reduce cognitive load. |
| 9 | Error recovery | Help user recover | **Example:** Drone control UI shows GPS-denied zones and offers automatic **return-to-home** as recovery. |
| 10 | Help & documentation | Provide searchable help | **CPS Extension:** Context-sensitive, **voice-activated** help (hands-free during surgery). |

*(Award 1 mark per row explained well; 7 marks for 7 rows.)*

---

**(b) Real-Time Feedback Architecture (7 Marks):**

**Block Diagram (textual representation):**

```
[Operator] → [Input Capture] → [Safety Validator] → [CPS Controller]
                                                                   ↓
[Display Update] ← [State Aggregator] ← [Physical Sensors] ← [Actuator]
```

**Step-by-step Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Identify control loop frequency:**

$$ f_c = 500 \text{ Hz} $$

**Step 2 — Compute control loop period:**

$$ T_c = \frac{1}{f_c} = \frac{1}{500} = 0.002 \text{ s} = 2 \text{ ms} $$

**Step 3 — Define Response Time Budget:**

The response time budget is the **total time** from operator action to displayed feedback:

$$ T_{\text{RTB}} = T_{\text{input}} + T_{\text{validate}} + T_{\text{compute}} + T_{\text{actuate}} + T_{\text{sense}} + T_{\text{display}} $$

**Step 4 — Apply the hard real-time constraint:**

$$ T_{\text{RTB}} \leq T_c = 2 \text{ ms} $$

**Step 5 — Apply 75% safety margin rule:**

$$ T_{\text{RTB,allocated}} \leq 0.75 \cdot T_c = 1.5 \text{ ms} $$

This leaves 0.5 ms (25%) headroom for **jitter, network delay, and computational spikes** — critical for hard real-time systems.

**Step 6 — Typical allocation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{input}} &= 0.2 \text{ ms} \\
T_{\text{validate}} &= 0.2 \text{ ms} \\
T_{\text{compute}} &= 0.3 \text{ ms} \\
T_{\text{actuate}} &= 0.3 \text{ ms} \\
T_{\text{sense}} &= 0.2 \text{ ms} \\
T_{\text{display}} &= 0.3 \text{ ms} \\
\hline
T_{\text{RTB}} &= 1.5 \text{ ms} \leq 2 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** For a 500 Hz robotic manipulator, the entire UI feedback pipeline must complete within **2 ms**, with **1.5 ms** as the practical budget after the safety margin.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **four-layer CPS usability engineering framework**. Describe the role of **cognitive load management** with reference to **Miller's Law** and the **stress factor $\alpha$**.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Compare and contrast the **accessibility requirements** of CPS interfaces with traditional web/desktop interfaces. Discuss how **WCAG 2.2** principles are extended to CPS modalities (visual, auditory, haptic, gesture).

---

**Model Answer for Question B:**

**(a) Four-Layer CPS Usability Framework (7 Marks):**

The four layers, from operator outward, are:

**Layer 1 — Cognitive Load Management (Closest to Operator):**
- Limits the number of information chunks displayed at any time.
- Based on Miller's Law: $W_m = 7 \pm 2$ chunks.
- Under CPS stress, working memory is reduced:
$$ W_m^{\text{CPS}} = \alpha \cdot W_m, \quad \alpha \approx 0.6 $$
- Therefore, under stress, $W_m^{\text{CPS}} \approx 4$ chunks — the UI must show **at most 4 critical items** simultaneously.
- Techniques: progressive disclosure, focus mode, situation-awareness displays.

**Layer 2 — Real-Time Feedback Architecture:**
- Ensures $T_{\text{RTB}} \leq T_c$ for all user actions.
- Uses 75% safety margin rule.
- Hardware-accelerated rendering (e.g., GPU-based HMI).

**Layer 3 — Safety-Critical Interaction Design:**
- Implements the three-state model (Safe / Caution / Unsafe).
- Uses IEC 61508 SIL classification.
- Includes e-stop with $T_{\text{estop}} \leq 100$ ms.

**Layer 4 — Accessibility & Universal Design (Outermost):**
- Adapts to operator capabilities: vision, hearing, motor, cognitive.
- Implements WCAG 2.2 extended to physical modalities.
- Provides multi-modal output and adaptive input.

**Role of Cognitive Load Management — Detailed:**

The cognitive load metric is:

$$ C_{\text{load}} = \frac{I}{\alpha \cdot W_m} $$

where $I$ is the number of distinct information elements displayed. The compliance rule is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{load}} \leq 0.6 &\Rightarrow \text{SAFE} \\
0.6 < C_{\text{load}} \leq 1.0 &\Rightarrow \text{CAUTION} \\
C_{\text{load}} > 1.0 &\Rightarrow \text{UNSAFE — redesign required}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example:** An aircraft cockpit traditionally displays 50+ dials. Modern "glass cockpits" (e.g., Boeing 787) consolidate these into 4–6 PFD/MFD screens, each showing ≤ 4 critical parameters — directly applying the CPS cognitive load rule.

*(Award 7 marks: 1 mark per layer + 3 marks for cognitive load derivation + 1 mark for example.)*

---

**(b) Accessibility in CPS vs Traditional Interfaces (7 Marks):**

**Comparison Table:**

| Dimension | Traditional Web/Desktop | CPS Interface |
|---|---|---|
| Primary user | Sighted, mouse/keyboard user | Operator with mixed modalities, often under stress |
| Time to act | Minutes | Milliseconds |
| Consequence of inaccessibility | User frustration | Physical harm / mission failure |
| Modalities | Mostly visual + audio | Visual, audio, haptic, gesture, voice, eye-tracking, EEG |
| Standards | WCAG 2.2 | WCAG 2.2 + IEC 61508 + ISO 26262 + domain standards |
| Adaptability | User-configurable (static) | **Real-time adaptive** (context + stress + role) |
| Failure mode | Page error | Loss of physical control |

**WCAG 2.2 Extended to CPS Modalities:**

1. **Perceivable — Visual:** Contrast ratio $\geq 4.5{:}1$; support for color-blind modes; large fonts for control rooms.
2. **Perceivable — Auditory:** Voice output with adjustable rate; tactile alerts (e.g., vibrating smartwatch for industrial workers).
3. **Perceivable — Haptic:** Vibration patterns to convey alarm severity (1 pulse = caution, 3 pulses = critical).
4. **Operable — Voice:** "Hey system, stop the conveyor" — supported in noisy factory floors via beamforming mics.
5. **Operable — Gesture:** Hand-tracking for sterile surgical environments; eye-tracking for pilots.
6. **Operable — Motor accessibility:** Tap targets $\geq 9 \text{ mm} \times 9 \text{ mm}$; voice fallback for users with motor impairment.
7. **Understandable — Cognitive:** Plain-language alarms; multilingual support; progressive disclosure for novice operators.
8. **Robust — Cross-platform:** Same accessibility features across HMI, mobile, AR glasses, and voice assistants.

**Conclusion:** CPS accessibility extends WCAG's visual-centric principles to **multi-modal, time-critical, and safety-critical** contexts, requiring **real-time adaptation** rather than static configuration.

*(Award 7 marks: 2 marks for comparison + 5 marks for WCAG extensions with examples.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing CPS HCI with Web HCI:** Students often write generic HCI answers. Always emphasize **real-time, safety, physical consequences**.
> 2. **Forgetting Miller's Law numerical value:** Always quote $7 \pm 2$ chunks and derive $W_m^{\text{CPS}} = 4$ under stress.
> 3. **Missing the Panic Button Principle:** Many students omit the **e-stop ≤ 100 ms** rule — a favourite KTU question.
> 4. **Skipping units and safety margins:** Always show $T_{\text{RTB}}$ with units (ms) and apply the 75% rule.
> 5. **Not linking WCAG to CPS modalities:** Don't stop at "WCAG is for the web" — explicitly map each principle to **haptic/gesture/voice**.
> 6. **Two-state vs Three-state confusion:** The **three-state safety model** is a CPS-specific concept worth 2–3 marks.
> 7. **Forgetting SIL/IEC 61508:** KTU examiners expect students to mention functional safety standards.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Must Memorize for KTU 2024):**

- ⭐ **Definition:** Usability in CPS = Effectiveness + Efficiency + Satisfaction + **Safety**.
- ⭐ **Miller's Law:** $W_m = 7 \pm 2$ chunks; under stress, $W_m^{\text{CPS}} \approx 4$ chunks.
- ⭐ **Cognitive Load Rule:** $C_{\text{load}} = I / (\alpha \cdot W_m) \leq 1$ for safe operation.
- ⭐ **Real-Time Constraint:** $T_{\text{RTB}} \leq T_c = 1/f_c$; apply **75% safety margin** for hard real-time.
- ⭐ **Emergency Stop Rule:** $T_{\text{estop}} \leq 100$ ms (1-tap, always reachable — the **Panic Button Principle**).
- ⭐ **Three-State Safety Model:** Safe / Caution / Unsafe — based on $R_{\text{risk}} \cdot S_{\text{SIL}}$ product.
- ⭐ **Nielsen's 10 Heuristics** must be **augmented** with CPS-specific examples (visibility of **physical** state, cross-modal consistency, focus mode, graceful degradation).
- ⭐ **Shneiderman's 8 Golden Rules** — know all 8 and at least 4 CPS adaptations.
- ⭐ **WCAG 2.2 four principles** — Perceivable, Operable, Understandable, Robust — extended to **multi-modal CPS** (haptic, gesture, voice, eye-tracking).
- ⭐ **Key Standards:** ISO 9241-11 (usability), IEC 61508 (functional safety), IEC 62366-1 (medical HMI), ISO 26262 (automotive), DO-178C (avionics).
- ⭐ **Universal Design (UD)** principles: Equitable use, Flexibility, Simple & Intuitive, Perceptible info, Tolerance for error, Low effort, Size & space.
- ⭐ **SUS Score** $\geq 68$ = average; $\geq 80$ = excellent for safety-critical CPS.
- ⭐ **Cross-Modal Consistency Drift:** Must be $\leq 50$ ms between voice/gesture/touch.
- ⭐ **Focus Mode:** Auto-hide non-critical info during alarms to reduce cognitive load.
- ⭐ **Graceful Degradation:** When sensors fail, **show partial data explicitly** — never hide missing data silently.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Line Mantra for KTU Viva:** *"In CPS, the UI is not a feature — it is a life-safety component. Real-time, accessible, and safe-by-design."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ensuring Accessibility for Diverse User Groups in CPS Interface Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT524 - INTERFACE DESIGN FOR CPS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Ensuring Accessibility for Diverse User Groups in CPS Interface Design # Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Accessibility in CPS Interface Design** is the discipline of designing, developing, and evaluating Cyber-Physical System interfaces such that the full range of human users — regardless of age, ability, disability, language, or environmental context — can **perceive, operate, understand, and robustly interact** with the system's computational and physical components on an **equitable basis**. In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, accessibility is treated as a **non-functional cross-cutting requirement** of CPS interface design, sitting alongside usability, safety, and security. It is governed by international standards such as: - **WCAG 2.2** (Web Content Accessibility Guidelines) — **4.5:1** minimum contrast ratio for normal text (Level AA) - **ISO 9241-171** — Ergonomics of human-system interaction, accessibility - **EN 301 549** — Accessibility requirements for ICT products and services - **Section 508** of the U.S. Rehabilitation Act (for reference) - **UN CRPD** (Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities) The cornerstone organizing principle used in KTU evaluations is **POUR** — **Perceivable, Operable, Understandable, Robust**. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — "The Curb Cut"**: Picture a city sidewalk. Originally, curbs were sharp right-angled edges designed for drainage. When wheelchair users and parents with strollers needed to cross, the city added the gently-sloped **curb cut**. That same curb cut now helps delivery carts, cyclists, tourists with luggage, and even skate-boarders. *Nobody* was made worse off, yet *many* were made dramatically better off. > > This is **accessibility**: designing the *primary* path so that the *widest possible* set of users reaches the *same destination* without needing a separate, second-class "accessible" route. > > In a **CPS interface** (say, a smart-home control panel), this means the same touchscreen that lets an able-bodied teenager dim the lights must also let a 70-year-old with arthritis, a blind user via voice, and a non-English-speaking migrant worker via icons do the *exact same thing* — without a separate "handicapped menu." ## 1.3 Why Accessibility Is a CPS-Specific Concern Unlike a pure software GUI, a CPS interface bridges: 1. **Digital layers** (HMI, dashboards, mobile apps, voice assistants) 2. **Physical layers** (sensors, actuators, embedded displays, wearables, vehicles, industrial robots) A failure in accessibility in CPS can cause **physical harm**, not just digital frustration. A blind user cannot hear a vibrating alarm on a smart insulin pump; a deaf user cannot hear an autonomous vehicle's horn; a user with motor impairment cannot escape the trajectory of a robotic arm quickly enough. This elevates accessibility from a "nice-to-have" to a **safety-critical design constraint**. ## 1.4 The Four POUR Principles — Quick Reference > [!IMPORTANT] > **POUR — The Foundational Accessibility Framework** > > | Principle | Question It Answers | CPS Example | > |---|---|---| > | **Perceivable** | Can the user *sense* the information? | Tactile + audio + visual alert on a medical CPS device | > | **Operable** | Can the user *act* on the interface? | Voice + gesture + button control for a smart-home CPS | > | **Understandable** | Can the user *comprehend* the information and operation? | Plain-language status messages on an industrial CPS dashboard | > | **Robust** | Will the interface *still work* with assistive tech and future updates? | Standard ARIA markup on a CPS web dashboard | These will be expanded in detail in **Section 2**. # Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The POUR Principles in Depth (with CPS Anchors) ### 2.1.1 Perceivable Information and interface components must be presented in ways users can perceive through **at least one available sensory channel**. - **Text alternatives** for non-text content (e.g., image-based sensor readings in a CPS dashboard must have alt-text or audio descriptions). - **Captions and transcripts** for audio alerts in CPS (e.g., fire alarm in a smart building). - **Adaptable content** — information must be presentable in different ways without losing meaning. - **Distinguishable** — adequate color contrast, text resizing up to **200%** without loss of functionality, audio control. ### 2.1.2 Operable Interface components and navigation must be operable by users with diverse motor and sensory capabilities. - **Keyboard accessible** — every function reachable without a mouse (critical for CPS with physical buttons or touchscreens). - **Enough time** — adjustable timeouts, ability to extend sessions (vital for elderly users on medical CPS). - **No seizure-inducing content** — flash limits below **3 Hz** (relevant for CPS visual alerts). - **Navigable** — clear page/screen titles, focus indicators, link purposes. - **Input modalities** — multi-modal input (touch, voice, gesture, switch, sip-and-puff). ### 2.1.3 Understandable Information and operation must be understandable to the user. - **Readable** — language level, abbreviations explained. - **Predictable** — consistent navigation, no unexpected context changes. - **Input assistance** — error prevention, labels, suggestions. ### 2.1.4 Robust Content must be robust enough to be interpreted reliably by a wide range of user agents, including current and future assistive technologies. - **Compatible** — use of valid, semantic markup (ARIA roles in CPS web dashboards). ## 2.2 Universal Design — The 7 Principles (Ronald Mace, 1997) > [!NOTE] > KTU examiners frequently test **Universal Design (UD)** alongside POUR. UD is a *design philosophy*, while POUR is a *technical compliance framework*. Both intersect heavily. 1. **Equitable Use** — Same means of use for all users; not segregating. 2. **Flexibility in Use** — Multiple modes of interaction. 3. **Simple and Intuitive** — Unnecessary complexity eliminated. 4. **Perceptible Information** — Communicates effectively to the user. 5. **Tolerance for Error** — Minimizes hazards of accidental actions. 6. **Low Physical Effort** — Operated with minimum fatigue. 7. **Size and Space for Approach and Use** — Adequate space regardless of body size, posture, mobility. ## 2.3 WCAG Conformance Levels | Level | Tag | Meaning | KTU Weight | |---|---|---|---| | **A** | Minimum | Removes *barriers* that prevent access | Tested in 3-mark questions | | **AA** | Standard (legal benchmark) | Removes *significant* barriers | **Most KTU exam focus** | | **AAA** | Enhanced | Removes barriers in specialized contexts | Mentioned for gold-standard CPS | ## 2.4 Assistive Technologies (AT) Relevant to CPS | AT Type | User Need Addressed | CPS Application | |---|---|---| | **Screen readers** (JAWS, NVDA, VoiceOver) | Blind / low-vision | CPS web dashboards, mobile apps | | **Screen magnifiers** (ZoomText) | Low-vision | Embedded industrial displays | | **Refreshable Braille displays** | Blind | Tactile CPS alarms | | **Voice recognition** (Dragon, Alexa) | Motor-impaired | Voice-controlled CPS actuators | | **Switch / Sip-and-puff** | Severe motor impairment | Wheelchair-mounted CPS controllers | | **Eye-tracking** | Motor-impaired (ALS) | Hands-free CPS operation | | **Haptic wearables** | Deaf / hard-of-hearing | Vibration alerts from CPS sensors | | **Captioning / Sign-language avatars** | Deaf | CPS video emergency alerts | ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following equations are the **mathematical backbone** examiners expect for accessibility evaluation questions. | # | Formula / Metric | LaTeX Form | Standard Threshold | Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Relative Luminance (sRGB)** | $L = 0.2126 \cdot R + 0.7152 \cdot G + 0.0722 \cdot B$ | $0 \leq L \leq 1$ | Contrast computation | | 2 | **sRGB Linearization (low)** | $R = R_{sRGB} / 12.92$ | For $R_{sRGB} \leq 0.03928$ | sRGB → linear | | 3 | **sRGB Linearization (high)** | $R = \left(\dfrac{R_{sRGB} + 0.055}{1.055}\right)^{2.4}$ | For $R_{sRGB} > 0.03928$ | sRGB → linear | | 4 | **WCAG Contrast Ratio** | $CR = \dfrac{L_{lighter} + 0.05}{L_{darker} + 0.05}$ | $CR \geq 4.5$ (AA normal text) $CR \geq 3.0$ (AA large text) $CR \geq 7.0$ (AAA) | Color accessibility | | 5 | **Fitts' Law — Movement Time** | $MT = a + b \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right)$ | $W \geq 24$ px (WCAG 2.2 AA) $W \geq 44$ px (AAA / Apple HIG) | Target size / motor accessibility | | 6 | **Index of Difficulty** | $ID = \log_{2}\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right)$ | Lower $ID$ = easier target | Haptic / touch target design | | 7 | **Effective Walking Speed (CPS pedestrian-aware)** | $v = d / t$ | $v_{typical} = 1.4$ m/s $v_{elderly} = 0.8$ m/s $v_{accessibility} = 0.6$ m/s | Autonomous vehicle CPS, smart-crosswalk | | 8 | **Stevens' Power Law (perception)** | $\psi(I) = k \cdot I^{a}$ | Different $a$ per sense (vision $a \approx 0.5$, hearing $a \approx 0.6$) | Multi-modal CPS alert scaling | | 9 | **Throughput (Fitts)** | $TP = \dfrac{ID}{MT}$ bits/s | Maximize $TP$ | Evaluating motor-accessible CPS UI | | 10 | **ISO 9241-9 Accuracy** | $A = 1 - \dfrac{\text{errors}}{\text{total\_trials}}$ | $A \geq 0.90$ | Accessibility testing | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Exam Tip:** When asked to "evaluate the accessibility of a color pair," examiners expect you to (i) linearize, (ii) compute $L$, (iii) apply the contrast formula, and (iv) state the WCAG level. Skipping any step costs marks. ## 2.6 Multi-Modal Interface Architecture in CPS A multi-modal interface provides **redundant sensory channels** so that loss of one channel (e.g., deafness) does not block the task. The canonical model is: $$I = \{I_{visual},\; I_{audio},\; I_{haptic},\; I_{gesture},\; I_{voice},\; I_{textual}\}$$ For accessibility, the **minimum redundancy rule** is that for any *critical* CPS state transition, **at least two independent modalities** must be activated. This is formalized as: $$M_{critical} = \{m_i, m_j \;\vert\; i \neq j,\; \text{channel}(m_i) \neq \text{channel}(m_j)\}$$ ## 2.7 Engineering Utility of These Models - **Automotive CPS** (autonomous vehicles): Contrast ratios govern dashboard legibility under glare; walking-speed models determine pedestrian-yield timing. - **Medical CPS** (insulin pumps, ventilators): Stevens' Power Law governs the loudness of escalation alarms; multi-modal redundancy prevents single-sensor disability accidents. - **Industrial CPS** (collaborative robots): Fitts' Law sizes the emergency-stop button; tolerance-for-error (UD principle 5) governs dead-man-switch logic. - **Smart-home CPS**: Voice-first + tactile-first design covers both blind and deaf users from the same code path. # Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix This section works through the **two most exam-relevant mathematical derivations** in CPS accessibility: the **WCAG Contrast Ratio** and **Fitts' Law for target sizing**. ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — WCAG Contrast Ratio (CR) ### 3.1.1 Setup You are given two sRGB color values, expressed as 8-bit channels (0–255): $$C_1 = (R_1, G_1, B_1), \quad C_2 = (R_2, G_2, B_2)$$ We must compute the contrast ratio $CR$ and determine if it passes WCAG 2.2 Level AA (≥ 4.5:1 for normal text). ### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Normalize sRGB Channels to [0, 1] Convert each 8-bit channel to a fraction: $$R_{sRGB} = \frac{R_8}{255}, \quad G_{sRGB} = \frac{G_8}{255}, \quad B_{sRGB} = \frac{B_8}{255}$$ ### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Linearize Each Channel The human eye perceives light non-linearly. sRGB compands the signal; we must undo it to get true linear light intensity. For a normalized value $V_{sRGB}$: $$ V = \begin{cases} \dfrac{V_{sRGB}}{12.92}, & \text{if } V_{sRGB} \leq 0.03928 \\[8pt] \left(\dfrac{V_{sRGB} + 0.055}{1.055}\right)^{2.4}, & \text{if } V_{sRGB} > 0.03928 \end{cases} $$ Apply independently to $R$, $G$, $B$ to obtain linear $(R, G, B)$. ### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Compute Relative Luminance $L$ Luminance is the perceived brightness weighted by the eye's spectral sensitivity (the **Rec. 709 / sRGB coefficients**): $$L = 0.2126 \cdot R + 0.7152 \cdot G + 0.0722 \cdot B$$ Note that **green dominates** because the human eye has the most cones in the M (green) region. ### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Compute the Contrast Ratio Let $L_{max} = \max(L_1, L_2)$ and $L_{min} = \min(L_1, L_2)$.
